title: Introduce telemetry-driven synthetic load generator and hyperops for batch profile autotuning
preview: Problem: Production telemetry indicates wide variance in per-customer request shape (seq lengths, prompt/templates, temperature) which makes static batching defaults suboptimal. We need a way to synthesize representative load from existing traces, run lightweight microbenchmark passes, and generate conservative batch/cache/quantization profiles that are safe for rollout. This PR implements a telemetry-driven 'synthetic-econ-proxy' tool plus runtime hyperops that drive an autotuner. Goals: 1) generate reproducible synthetic streams from sampled traces, 2) run a fast hyperparameter sweep for batch sizes, microbatch windows, and cache TTLs in a sandbox mode, 3) attach a compact 'handoff' metadata blob to candidate profiles for safe staged rollout and automatic rollback. Key features: - synthetic-econ-proxy CLI: consumes sampled telemetry (p50/p95 seq distribution, client headers, model family) and emits a deterministic request stream for local or staged perf runs. - hyperops runner: lightweight orchestrator that runs short trials across candidate batch/microbatch/kv-cache policies and collects cost/latency metrics. - profile handoff format: single-line compact metadata (versioned tag, checksum, compatibility hints, suggested fallback) used by the runtime to decide compatible rollouts. - safety: candidate profiles are generated with conservative SLO buffers and include automatic fallback to prior profile on SLA regressions. - developer UX: preview command (redwoodctl perf preview --profile <file>) that replays the synthetic stream against a chosen model variant and prints unit economics estimates. Motivation and context: This is an engineering-first approach to reduce human iteration when choosing defaults for new customers or workloads. Rather than relying on a single scoring heuristic, we synthesize a workload closest to the customer's telemetry and run a controlled sweep to find economically sensible defaults. See linked Linear items and test harness in the commits. Checklist: - [x] synthetic stream generator - [x] hyperops runner and metric collectors - [x] compact profile handoff format and compatibility checks - [x] redwoodctl preview command + CLI docs - [x] unit and integration tests - [x] e2e smoke runs on staging - [x] changelog/release notes Comments: This PR purposefully keeps quantization changes limited to attaching quant 'hints' to profiles rather than forcing conversion; quantization conversions remain an explicit operator action. The approach avoids large runtime changes and hooks into existing rollout machinery.

title: Self-healing traffic gate with prognostic rollback scheduler
preview: Motivation: We observed incident patterns where a small regression in an A/B arm degraded user experience slowly and only surfaced after sustained traffic. This PR adds a prognostic rollback scheduler and a self-healing traffic gate that combines short-horizon telemetry forecasting, a conservative anomaly scorer, and lightweight replay/bisect workflows so the system can proactively hold or revert risky promotes before widespread impact.  Summary of changes: implemented a prognostic score pipeline that consumes recent SLO/time-series windows and outputs a short-term risk forecast; added TrafficGate controller that enforces holdpoints when forecasted risk crosses policy thresholds; built a rescue-oracle that can run fast replay of recent 1% samples in a sandbox to validate rollback decisions; added operator-visible "rescue" and "replay" actions to Redwood Console so on-call can review diagnostics and trigger manual rescue if needed; introduced feature-flagging and gradual enablement config under rollout.policy.prognostic.enabled=false by default.  Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-4421) [x] unit + integration tests for forecast scorer [x] smoke tests for traffic gate hold/open actions [x] e2e replay against local-capture harness [x] console UI surfaced and smoke validated  Implementation notes: forecasting uses a lightweight ARIMA-like exponential smoother tuned for short windows to avoid model drift; anomaly scorer penalizes latency regressions higher than minor fidelity deltas; the rescue-oracle runs isolated synchronous replay (max 30s) and returns a scalar pass/fail + diff snapshot used to justify automated rollback.  Rollout plan: behind feature flag in main, enable in staging for 48h with canary percent=2% then roll to 10% with manual checks; enable prognostic auto-hold only after 7d stable metrics.  Related: supports both canary and A/B lanes and hooks into existing rollout audit logs and snapshotting.  Why different: unlike prior reactive rollback controllers, this one uses short-term prognostic signals and a bounded replay verification step to reduce false-positive auto-rollbacks.  Links: Linear ENG-4421 (design), SUP-1723 (support ticket that motivated fast replay).

title: per-request graceful-degradation: kernel compatibility shim + precision-hint API
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see tail latency regressions in mixed-precision kernels when models with large kv-cache footprints are routed through the 'fast' execution path. This PR introduces a lightweight compatibility shim and a per-request 'precision hint' header that lets the router/scheduler choose a conservative execution mode for requests that prioritize correctness over absolute token throughput.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a kernel-compat shim layer in the runtime that detects binary incompatibility or quantization sensitivity for a model+request combination and exposes a low-overhead boolean that the scheduler uses to avoid aggressive fusion/quantization fast-paths; implements an opt-in HTTP header (X-Redwood-Precision-Hint: prefer-correct | prefer-fast | neutral) and SDK flags that map to the same hint; extends continuous-batching logic to respect per-request hints by routing hinted requests to a separate lightweight queue that favors conservative kernels and smaller batch coalescing; introduces a KV-cache safety check that prevents prefix-optimized eviction heuristics from running for hinted requests; updates the metrics to surface how many requests used the compatibility shim and the delta in p50/p95 latency and token cost; includes tests and performance microbenchmarks.

Design notes and trade-offs: The shim is intentionally minimal: it does not change model weights or quantize on the fly. Instead it flips kernel-selection heuristics (no aggressive fused attention kernels, prefer fp16-safe paths, disable some prefetch transforms) and reduces batch coalescing windows to reduce peak memory pressure on hinted requests. This increases per-token cost but avoids correctness edge-cases introduced by certain fused kernels (observed in edge-case tests with malformed KV cache shapes). We chose a header + SDK-first API because it is the least invasive while enabling customers and platform teams to opt-in per-call.

Migration and rollout: The feature is behind a server-side flag (runtime.enable_precision_hint_shim). Default is off for all clusters; enabled in staging during canary; default for some internal teams with model reg tests. Rollout plan attached in the checklist.

Checklist: - [x] shim implementation and unit tests - [x] SDK header plumbing (python + ts) - [x] scheduler queue change and e2e tests - [x] add metrics and dashboard panels - [x] perf microbenchmarks (attached) - [x] rollout gating flag and docs

Related: Linear: ENG-4280, ENG-4297. Incident: SUP-1223 (customer reported hallucination/regression after aggressive kernel upgrade). More context: see internal doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4280/precision-hint-design (internal link).

Notes for reviewers: focus on runtime/shim correctness (no changed behavior for default-off), scheduler behavior (deadlocks or starvation between two queues), and SDK ergonomics. The PR intentionally avoids changing model catalog metadata until we validate metrics from canary.

Release notes: Adds an opt-in per-request precision hint header and a runtime compatibility shim to favor correctness over fused-kernel throughput.

title: Probabilistic length cohorter with pad-aware scheduling and KV-prefill heuristics
preview:
Motivation: long-tail sequence length variance continues to drive excessive padding and suboptimal kernel utilization in our continuous batching pipeline. Simple bucketing reduces padding but creates brittle boundaries and poor utilization when per-slot latency SLOs are tight. This PR introduces a probabilistic length cohorter that (1) forms soft cohorts by sampling length-affinity probabilities instead of hard buckets, (2) attaches a pad-cost estimate to each pending request using a small learned linear cost model, and (3) integrates KV-prefill priority to bias cohorts toward requests that benefit from warm KV caches. The goal is to reduce aggregate padding waste while keeping P95 latency within SLO and increasing kernel lane alignment for improved throughput.

Approach: the cohorter computes a length-affinity vector for each request based on requested max_tokens, recent sequence length distribution from the request's token histogram, and a lightweight token-density predictor. Requests are then assigned to cohorts probabilistically (tempered softmax) rather than deterministically; the cohort scheduler runs an acceptance policy that weighs cohort fill-level against estimated pad cost and KV-prefill benefit. The pad-cost estimator is a tiny ridge-regression model trained on our production trace snapshots (serialized model included under benchmarks/). KV-prefill heuristics mark requests with hot-key overlap to get a small scheduling boost so that cohorts end up with multiple warm-KV requests more often, reducing expensive cold-KV misses during generation. Integration points: runtime/batching/cohorter, runtime/scheduler/policy, runtime/kv/prefill, and a new bench harness under benchmarks/batch-sim.

Results: microbenchmarks on a 4xA100 pool and production-like trace show median pad tokens per request down 28% and average per-token GPU time improvement of 6% for multi-tenant traces. End-to-end P95 latency increased by 2% in worst-case stress tests but stayed within our target SLO for dedicated capacity. Cost-per-output-token decreased ~8% in the synthetic workload and ~3-5% on several customer traces. See benchmarks/summary.csv for full breakdown. We also measured improved kernel lane alignment (measured as contiguous lane occupancy metric) up 9% which maps to fewer kernel launches in multi-lane kernels.

Checklist: 1) unit tests for cohorter assignment and pad-cost estimator included, 2) integration test for scheduler with KV-prefill, 3) bench harness and dataset snapshot included, 4) flag guarded rollout (feature flag: probabilistic_cohorter) and runtime config knobs (temperature, pad_cost_weight, kv_prefill_boost), 5) docs RFC added. Backwards compatibility: default feature flag is OFF; old deterministic bucketer remains unchanged unless flag enabled. No API changes.

How to test locally: run 'make bench-cohorter' to reproduce microbenchmarks; run integration tests 'pytest tests/integration/test_cohorter.py::TestPrefillBias'. Deployment plan: staged rollout behind feature flag; enable for 5% of traffic on dedicated clusters for 48 hours, validate P95 and pad metrics in console dashboards; increase to 100% if stable.

Related: follows from ENG-7123 (experimental pad-cost model) and complements PRs for kv-prefill routing. Linear: ENG-8852. Notes: tuned default temperature=0.4 and pad_cost_weight=0.7 after ablations; conservative defaults chosen to avoid regressing latency-sensitive customers.

title: silence-probe-reconnect-backfill-and-cancel-deadline-enforcement
preview: Motivation: Customers observed client disconnects that left server-side streaming state in a limbo where tool-calls were retried and KV-cache backfills were skipped, causing higher token costs and functional duplication. This PR introduces a 'silence probe' reconciliation path, a reconnect probe that attempts lightweight session health checks on client reconnects, an explicit cancel-deadline enforcement for in-flight tool/function calls, and safer backfill semantics when reconnect resumes. Summary of changes: 1) server: add silence-probe detection that marks streams as 'candidate-orphan' after configurable idle windows and enqueues a lightweight snapshot probe before evicting; 2) reconnect: on new client handshake include reconnect-probe phase which validates KV-cache continuity and replays missing prefix tokens if safe; 3) cancel-deadline: tool invocation subsystem now tracks cancel deadlines separately from client websocket state and enforces idempotent abort semantics with explicit response code for clients; 4) backfill behavior: when resuming, we prefer prefix-reconciliation over blind backfill and record a short reconciliation trace to observability; 5) OpenAI-compat: adapter layer now maps the reconnect-probe handshake into a 409/202-style flow when applicable and preserves function-call idempotency tokens. Implementation notes: code changes touch the session manager, rpc/stream controller, toolcall executor, and the OpenAI compatibility adapter. Default timeouts are conservative (idle->candidate-orphan = 45s, probe timeout = 5s, cancel-deadline grace = 2s) with knobs exposed in config. Tests: added unit tests for probe state transitions, e2e integration test that simulates abrupt client disconnect and reconnect with toolcalls, and property tests for idempotent abort on repeated cancel signals. Safety: probe performs non-destructive validation only and will not execute user-visible toolcalls during reconciliation. Rollout plan: feature gated via runtime flag 'streaming.silence_probe_v1' with spreadsheet of customers to flip; metrics added: stream.probe.started, stream.probe.success, stream.resume.reconciled, toolcall.abort.forced; alerting suggested on probe.failures > 1% over 10m. Backwards compatibility: existing client behavior unchanged unless server flag enabled. Related: follows ENG-4821 and references earlier streaming incident INT-998. Breaking change: none. See release_notes for end-user impact.

title: ack-window-coalescing-and-reconnect-probe-for-resumable-streams
preview:
Context: We have had intermittent production incidents where client disconnect followed by rapid reconnects created duplicate tool/function calls or left server-side inflight work stranded. This PR introduces a lightweight client ACK window and a reconnect probe handshake to make streaming sessions more resilient to flapping connections while preserving OpenAI compatibility for client-driven cancels.

Goals:
- Allow the client to acknowledge receipt of terminal tokens (finalized structured output / function call invocation) so the server can safely drop inflight state sooner and avoid double-execution on reconnects.
- Coalesce rapid client cancel/soft-abort signals within a short ACK window to reduce noisy cancellation churn during flaky networks.
- Add a reconnect probe (small idempotency-aware handshake) that validates resume tokens and server lease state before resuming expensive work (tool calls/KV-cache continuations).
- Maintain compatibility with current OpenAI-style cancel/close semantics — no change required for most clients.

What changed (high-level):
- Protocol: add optional "ack_window_ms" header on initial stream request (default 3000ms). During the ack window the server accepts a single cancel token and will delay aggressive resource reclamation for that window; repeated cancels within the window are treated as coalesced (no new cancel events emitted to tool call layer).
- Protocol: introduce a short probe exchange during reconnect: client sends a lightweight probe with last-seen sequence id and resume_token; server responds with probe_ack and explicit lease_state (active/draining/expired). Resume proceeds only on probe_ack==ok and lease_state==active (or draining with explicit allow_resume flag).
- Server: tracking of client-ack receipts for final structured outputs. When a client ACK for a terminal event is received, the server will mark the session checkpointed and allow immediate resource reclamation without waiting for full TTL. This avoids duplicate tool invocations on quick reconnects.
- Tool/fn calling: calls are now annotated with an idempotency fingerprint (derived from call_id + resume_seq) and the runtime consults a short-lived dedupe cache keyed by that fingerprint. The cache lifetime is aligned to the ack_window + server_drain_timeout to balance dedup protection with memory usage.
- SDKs/compat: no breaking API change for typical streaming use. New optional headers are supported by Redwood's OpenAI-compat adapter; we added transparent fallbacks for clients that don't send the new fields.

Implementation notes:
- Added stream_ack.go in internal/runtime to centralize ack/coalescing logic and metrics.
- Modified openai/compat adapter to parse ack_window_ms and to run the reconnect probe flow on connection resumptions.
- Added a short-lived in-memory dedupe cache with metrics and an adaptive eviction timer. For Dedicated/Private, cache can be backed by local ephemeral Redis if configured (opt-in).

Testing and validation:
- Unit tests for ack coalescing, probe handshake, and dedupe cache eviction.
- Integration test that simulates client disconnect/reconnect flapping and asserts no duplicate tool calls and that structured output is only emitted once.
- Load test scenario: 10k streams with randomized disconnect windows to measure overhead; results show <=0.6% p99 token latency increase and 18% reduction in duplicate tool calls under flapping scenarios.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc linked (ENG-912)
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Backwards compatible adapter changes
- [x] Metrics/instrumentation (stream.ack.* and stream.probe.*)
- [x] E2E flapping load test results attached

Rollout plan:
- Merge behind feature flag stream_ack_coalesce (off by default).
- Enable for 10% of hosted traffic for 24h; monitor stream.probe.failures and tool.duplicate.detected.
- Enable SDK opt-in examples in quickstarts after 10% ramp is healthy.

Notes/Tradeoffs:
- Acknowledgement window delays aggressive server TTL reclamation; chosen default (3s) is conservative — short enough to limit resource ties, long enough to absorb network blips.
- Dedup cache increases memory pressure slightly; adaptive eviction mitigates long tails. For Dedicated customers we provide config knobs to tune ext cache.

Related/precedent: several internal incident postmortems (SUP-428) drove this work. This complements prior resumable-handshake PRs but focuses on coalescing/cancellation semantics and an explicit probe phase to gate resume onto server lease health.

- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Backwards compatible adapter changes
- [x] Metrics/instrumentation (stream.ack.* and stream.probe.*)

title: model-catalog: add filters, compatibility flags, and pricing/limits fields to /models API
preview: Summary: Adds richer filtering and capability metadata to the public model catalog endpoints. This PR introduces query filters for capability-based discovery and model attributes, plus explicit compatibility flags and first-class pricing/limits fields on model descriptors. Motivation: customers have asked for a machine-readable way to pick models by capability (e.g., tool-calling, structured output, streaming-stable) and to programmatically surface cost/limit information for routing and billing. The current /models response is sparse and requires ad-hoc parsing of descriptions in downstream services. What this changes: 1) API surface: GET /v1/models now accepts new query params: 'capability' (comma-separated values: chat, completion, embedding, tool_calling, structured_output, streaming), 'openai_compat' (true|false), 'pricing_tier' (free|standard|premium), and 'min_max_tokens' (min,max). Example: GET /v1/models?capability=tool_calling,structured_output&openai_compat=true 2) Response schema additions: each model object gains optional fields: 'capabilities' (string[]), 'compatibility_flags' (string[]; e.g. "openai_chat", "openai_completion", "openai_embeddings"), 'pricing' (object with flat_rate, per_token_input_usd, per_token_output_usd, currency), and 'limits' (object: max_tokens, max_requests_per_minute, concurrent_requests). These are optional to preserve backwards compatibility; existing clients will continue to work if they ignore new fields. 3) OpenAI compatibility adapter: adapter maps compatibility_flags to legacy OpenAI-style fields for the OpenAI compatibility layer. Added translation layer so openai_compat=true returns aliases expected by older clients. 4) Server-side indexing: index added for capability tags to keep catalog query latency within SLOs (see perf notes below). 5) Docs & SDK: OpenAPI spec updated, python/ts SDKs surface the new fields and query helpers. Compatibility/migration notes: fields are additive and optional; no required fields were changed. The only risk is downstream code that relies on the previous string parsing of model descriptions — we've added an integration test and a migration note in docs. Rollout plan: staged rollout with feature flag 'model_catalog_caps_v1'; default enabled for beta accounts and disabled for strict compat customers. Monitoring: new metrics added: model_catalog.filter_latency_ms, model_catalog.cache_hit_rate, and pricing_field_populated_ratio. Related: Linear ENG-1729 (catalog filter work), PM-404 (pricing requirements). Checklist: - [x] API schema and OpenAPI docs updated - [x] Server implementation of filters and response enrichment - [x] OpenAI compatibility adapter translation layer - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] SDK updates (python + ts) - [x] Perf benchmark added to benchmark-lab - [x] Docs + release notes Notes: see inline snippets below for example response and query. Example response snippet (illustrative): {"id":"rw-mini-1","name":"rw-mini","capabilities":["chat","embedding"],"compatibility_flags":["openai_chat","openai_embeddings"],"pricing":{"per_token_input_usd":"0.00001","per_token_output_usd":"0.00002","currency":"USD"},"limits":{"max_tokens":"4096","max_requests_per_minute":"600","concurrent_requests":"10"}}

title: Introduce kernel-level heatmap and cost-flow annotator for invocation traces
preview: Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to surface GPU/kernel-level queuing and contention hotspots correlated to token-level cost and per-stage latency. This PR adds a sampled, lightweight kernel-heatmap export and a cost-flow annotator that stitches stage timing with kernel occupancy to help SREs and runtime engineers diagnose expensive invocation patterns. Design summary: 1) A new kernel heatmap sampler emits compact histograms of kernel queue wait and execution time per-device and per-invocation-phase; sampling is prioritized for requests that exceed a configurable latency or cost threshold. 2) A cost-flow annotator postprocesses traces to attach a per-invocation-stage cost attribution vector (model load, key-value cache miss penalties, kernel stalls, token-generation time) and synthesizes rollup markers used by dashboards. 3) Minimal on-path overhead: sampler uses atomic ring-buffers and amortized aggregation at a local telemetry worker; annotation is done asynchronously by the trace aggregator to avoid adding tail latency. Implementation details: Added a new runtime probe (runtime/probes/kernel_sampler.cc) with export hooks to the observability pipeline, a proto schema (telemetry/protos/kernel_heatmap.proto) and a canonical generator for the annotator (observability/annotators/cost_flow_annotator.go). Also updated the console dashboards (dashboards/infra/kernel-heatmap-panel.json and dashboards/infra/cost-flow-explorer.json) and the SLO playbook (runbooks/kernel-contention.md). Rollout plan: feature gated behind observability.kernel_heatmap flag, disabled by default for existing customers. Canary enabled for internal clusters and a stage behind the rollout gate; follow-on step will enable 0.1% sampling for hosted tier and 1% for dedicated, then iterate. Backfill: included a small backfill job (observability/scripts/backfill_heatmap.py) to index recent traces for correlation testing. Testing: unit tests for sampler ring-buffer and proto serialization, integration test that validates annotations appear on traces end-to-end, and a perf microbenchmark showing <1.3% median p99 increase when sampling at 0.1% on benchmark lab. Migration/rollback: feature flag removal and a short migration script to drop the temporary heatmap index if disabled. Related: ENG-5421 (investigation into GPU contention during long prompts), ties to runbook playbooks introduced in previous telemetry work.

title: per-tenant throughput SLA enforcer + shared burst token refactor
preview:
Motivation: customers on Dedicated ask for predictable per-tenant throughput guarantees while still allowing short-term bursts across a shared fleet. This PR implements an in-runtime per-tenant throughput SLA enforcer plus a refactor of the fleet-wide shared burst token pool to support weighted allocation and backpressure coordination with the autoscaler.

Summary of changes: added a light-weight controller (runtime/fleet/sla_enforcer.go) that tracks per-tenant token budgets (tokens/sec) and allocates from the local host bucket first, then from the shared burst pool when available. The shared burst pool has been moved out of the legacy global dispatcher and into a refactored service (runtime/fleet/burst_pool.go) that exposes an async lease API and utilization metrics. The autoscaler now consumes burst-pool pressure signals to pre-warm capacity when pool exhaustion is sustained. Console and metrics surfaces updated to show per-tenant SLA usage and pool utilization. Documentation and runbook include guidance for setting tenant SLAs and fallback behavior.

Behavior and guarantees: tenants configured with a throughput_sla (rps/tokens-per-sec equivalent) will be rate-limited at the runtime layer; transient bursts up to configured burst_quota are served from the shared pool subject to per-tenant weight and global fairness. When the pool is exhausted for >2s for a tenant, the SLA enforcer starts returning a structured quota_exceeded error with an RFC-structured JSON body that downstream services can use for graceful degradation. Autoscaler integration: a new metric (burst_pool_pressure_ratio) is emitted and used by the Dedicated autoscaler to trigger proactive pre-warm scaling when sustained >75% for >30s.

Backward compatibility: all behaviors default to existing admission rules when no per-tenant SLA is configured. The shared pool refactor maintains previous heuristics but exposes knobs for weight and max_shares per tenant. Migration is guarded behind runtime feature flag "enable_sla_enforcer" (off by default in existing installs).

Testing: unit tests for lease accounting and weight arithmetic added; integration tests simulate multi-tenant burst scenarios and assert observability metrics and autoscaler hooks; e2e Canary harness included under perf-canary to validate latency/p99 under mixed workloads.

Related items: linked Linear tasks and runbook updates are referenced below. The PR is intentionally scoped to runtime and orchestration layers; model-serving kernels are unchanged.

Notes: this PR contains a couple of nontrivial refactors; reviewers, please pay attention to concurrency in burst_pool.go and to the metric tagging in runtime/telemetry.

title: add-invoke-slice-sampler-and-token-cascade-explainer
preview: Motivation: long-tail token cost and latency noise make it hard to correlate bursts to kernel warmup, prefix vs completion cost, and scheduling contention. This PR introduces two complementary observability primitives: (1) an Invoke Slice Sampler that slices invocations into time-aligned 'slices' to preserve bursty token-attribution while dramatically reducing cardinality and storage cost; and (2) a Token Cascade Explainer that attributes per-token cost/latency to a small set of causal contributors (prefix KV hits/misses, kernel warmup penalty, batching wait, and I/O stalls) and exposes an explainer panel in the console. Summary of changes: - New sampler: telemetry/ingest/slice_sampler.go adds a deterministic slice keyed by (tenant, route, invocation_bucket) and emits sampled span anchors plus compact slice digests. Sampler is opt-in via telemetry.config.slice_sampling.enabled=false|true. - Token cascade: pkg/observability/token_cascade.go implements cascade attribution heuristics that correlate per-token timing with kernel occupancy metadata and KV cache events; exposes cascade tags on spans (cascade.stage and cascade.score). - Dashboards: dashboards/invocation_cascade_explainer.json adds a new panel with per-slice token waterfall, kernel warmup heatmap, and slice-level cost breakdown (prefix vs completion vs kernel). - Ingest/transform: telemetry/transform/slice_hydrator.go and telemetry/transform/cascade_enricher.go perform lightweight join of sampled anchors to full spans to produce rollups. - Docs & examples: docs/observability/token-cascade.md and examples/observability/quickstart-slice.sh. - Tests: tests/e2e/observability/synthetic_slice_test.py adds deterministic workload that reproduces warmup-burst patterns and validates attribution. Design notes and rollout: the slice sampler reduces telemetry volume by ~12x on our internal bursty trace workloads; we configured a conservative default sampling rate of 1/100 slices per tenant but added adaptive rate increases for tenants with high SLO sensitivity. The cascade explainer is purely additive instrumentation and will appear as optional panels; it does not change any runtime decision logic. Backwards compatibility: existing span shapes and tags remain unchanged unless telemetry.config.cascade_enrichment.enabled=true. Rollout plan: feature flag gated, staged rollout over 2 weeks: opt-in in staging, 10% of production tenants, 50% after stability checks, full rollout. Testing: unit tests for sampler determinism, integration tests for hydration path, and e2e synthetic replay. Checklist: - [x] implement sampler and hydrator - [x] add cascade attribution heuristics - [x] new dashboard panels and docs - [x] synthetic e2e tests - [x] feature-flag gating and config defaults Performance notes: measured ~0.8ms median overhead added to ingest path when enrichment enabled (95th percentile spikes at 5ms during backfill). Storage cost impact: with default sampling we saw ~12x reduction in sampled-span volume on internal traces, with cascade rollups adding ~3% storage. Related: ENG-6591 (observability long-tail), SUP-4121 (customer-reported burst attribution request).

title: Orchestra pausepoint harness with transparent rewind and cohort quarantine
preview:
Motivation:
We need a deterministic way to hold, inspect, and safely rewind model rollout traffic for live canaries and A/B experiments without losing session-affinity or producing silent regressions. Current pause mechanics are ad-hoc (manual scaling, pod cordon) and opaque in telemetry which makes post-hoc root cause and safe rewind labor-intensive.

What this PR does (summary):
- Introduces a Pausepoint state machine inside the orchestrator that exposes explicit holdpoints for staged rollouts. Holdpoints can be triggered by policy (telemetry gates) or manually from the Console.
- Implements a Cohort Quarantine controller that isolates affected user slices when a holdpoint is entered; quarantined cohorts are routed to a shadow or golden variant, and traffic is stamped for replay.
- Adds Transparent Rewind: deterministic session-preserving replay of quarantined traffic against previous model snapshots with side-by-side scoring so operators can compare deltas before committing an automatic revert.
- Expands telemetry: every pausepoint decision now emits a decision_reason tag, cohort_id, and a deterministic trace id that ties replay sessions to original requests.
- Includes operator runbook and console UI for inspecting pausepoint state, kicking off a rewind, or escalating to manual rollback.

Design notes:
- Pausepoint lifecycle: OPEN -> HOLDING -> QUARANTINED -> REPLAYING -> RESOLVED (COMMIT/REVERT). Transitions are guarded by timeouts and safety tokens so long-running holds can be auto-escalated.
- Quarantine semantics: quarantined cohorts are isolated at routing layer; their requests are mirrored to both candidate and golden models. Mirrors are rate-limited and tagged for replay capture.
- Rewind integrity: replay is performed preserving session cookies and KV cache pointers when available; where exact replay is impossible (external side-effects) we mark the trace as non-deterministic and exclude it from automatic revert decisions.

Checklist:
- [x] Pausepoint state machine + controller implementation
- [x] Cohort quarantine routing + mirror capture
- [x] Deterministic replay harness for transparent rewind
- [x] Telemetry tags and Console UI for pausepoint inspection
- [x] Integration tests covering holdpoint flow and replay verification
- [x] Runbook and operator playbook for incident handling

Backward compatibility:
- Pausepoints are opt-in via a new CRD (Pausepoint) and a feature flag. Default behavior for existing rollouts is unchanged.

Risks and mitigations:
- Increased memory footprint for mirrored requests: mitigated with streaming mirrors and configurable retention TTLs.
- Replay non-determinism could produce spurious signals: we surface non-deterministic flags and exclude affected traces from automated scoring.

How to test locally:
1) bootstrap local orchestrator with feature flag enabled (scripts/local_replay_fixture.sh)
2) create a Pausepoint CR using examples/rollouts/cohort_quarantine.yml
3) trigger a canary and send test traffic; use Console PausepointPanel to enter HOLD state
4) initiate Replay and watch telemetry spans emitted to local Jaeger

Related work and tickets: ENG-3245 (pausepoint design), ENG-3310 (transparent rewind prototype), SUP-4421 (customer incident: noisy canary requiring manual quarantine)

Notes on scope: This change focuses on rollout control-plane and routing glue; it does not attempt to modify model runtime kernels or KV cache serialization across versions — those are future follow-ups (linked in design doc).

title: shortburst-attention-fusion-and-slab-packing-for-short-sequence-latency
preview: Motivation: Many customer workloads at Redwood are dominated by short sequences (<64 tokens) where per-request overhead (kernel launch, KV cache management, micro-batch alignment) dominates token cost. This PR introduces a runtime path optimized for short-burst workloads: 1) an attention-fusion kernel variant that fuses small-sequence attention + rotary/ALiBi precompute to reduce kernel launches for sequences <= 64, 2) a token-slab packing allocator that packs multiple short requests into a single device slab with per-request offsets to avoid per-token KV allocations, and 3) a micro-continuous batching tweak that lowers the batching tick for short sequences while preserving overall throughput for long sequences. The goal is to reduce median latency for short requests without regressions for long-tail workloads. Changes: implemented fused_attention_short.cu and refactored attention dispatch to pick the fused kernel based on sequence_length and head_count thresholds; added slab_packer.{cc,h} which provides a fast arena allocator for short KV segments and a lightweight header describing offsets; introduced a new shortburst batching policy that lowers the flush timeout to 2ms for short requests and adds an adaptive cooldown to avoid microbursting on sustained high QPS; added smoke and microbench tests simulating tens of thousands of small requests. Compatibility: default behavior is conservative—the fused kernel engages only when model geometry matches supported head/hidden layouts and when quantization mode is FP16 or 4-bit fused path; slab packing is opt-in via runtime cfg short_slab_enabled=true to allow staged rollouts. Rollout plan: canary on dedicated-pool-3 for 48 hours, metric watch on median_p95_latency_shortseq, kv_allocator_misses, gpu_util; automatic rollback on error spikes. This PR also includes a short note for SDK teams to add a small-client-side retry jitter to avoid synchronized flapping. text: Motivation: Many customer workloads at Redwood are dominated by short sequences (<64 tokens) where per-request overhead (kernel launch, KV cache management, micro-batch alignment) dominates token cost. This PR introduces a runtime path optimized for short-burst workloads: 1) an attention-fusion kernel variant that fuses small-sequence attention + rotary/ALiBi precompute to reduce kernel launches for sequences <= 64, 2) a token-slab packing allocator that packs multiple short requests into a single device slab with per-request offsets to avoid per-token KV allocations, and 3) a micro-continuous batching tweak that lowers the batching tick for short sequences while preserving overall throughput for long sequences. The goal is to reduce median latency for short requests without regressions for long-tail workloads. Changes: implemented fused_attention_short.cu and refactored attention dispatch to pick the fused kernel based on sequence_length and head_count thresholds; added slab_packer.{cc,h} which provides a fast arena allocator for short KV segments and a lightweight header describing offsets; introduced a new shortburst batching policy that lowers the flush timeout to 2ms for short requests and adds an adaptive cooldown to avoid microbursting on sustained high QPS; added smoke and microbench tests simulating tens of thousands of small requests. Compatibility: default behavior is conservative—the fused kernel engages only when model geometry matches supported head/hidden layouts and when quantization mode is FP16 or 4-bit fused path; slab packing is opt-in via runtime cfg short_slab_enabled=true to allow staged rollouts. Rollout plan: canary on dedicated-pool-3 for 48 hours, metric watch on median_p95_latency_shortseq, kv_allocator_misses, gpu_util; automatic rollback on error spikes. This PR also includes a short note for SDK teams to add a small-client-side retry jitter to avoid synchronized flapping. Checklist: [x] unit tests added for slab allocator and fused kernel dispatch [x] microbench harness for short-seq latency [x] docs: runtime tuning note in perf/short_seq.md [x] Linear ticket: ENG-1842.

title: Add hardware-timestamp tracepoints and synthetic-replay microbench for kernel latency and tile occupancy
preview: Motivation: pinpoint sub-kernel latency variance and per-tile occupancy skew seen during continuous batching on long sequences. This PR adds lightweight hardware-timestamp tracepoints around kernel launches, a small lock-free per-tile occupancy counter, and a deterministic synthetic-replay microbenchmark that replays recorded request traces at controlled concurrency for stable profiling. Changes are gated behind runtime flags so there is zero overhead when disabled. High-level summary: 1) runtime/tracing/hw_timestamp_trace.h/.cc implements RAII-style trace scopes that capture wallclock and GPU hw timestamps when available and annotate the internal timeline with span ids, 2) runtime/scheduler/tile_counter.h/.cc introduces a cache-friendly per-tile occupancy counter sampled by the scheduler to record occupancy distributions, 3) tools/replay_bench parses a compressed request trace and replays with configurable arrival patterns and concurrency to exercise the batching scheduler reproducibly, 4) observability/exporters/perf_dashboard_exporter.py converts trace output into a bundled JSON for the perf dashboard including per-kernel latency histograms and per-tile occupancy heatmaps, 5) added unit and microbench tests and CI job to validate reproducible runs. Checklist: - [x] add hw timestamp trace scopes - [x] implement per-tile occupancy counters - [x] implement synthetic replay bench - [x] export summarized metrics for dashboard - [x] add CI microbench job - [x] update docs and example traces. Usage notes: enable tracing with REDWOOD_TRACING=hw_timestamp or use the scheduler flag --enable-tile-counters. Replay tool accepts --trace-file and --concurrency flags. Recorded traces are compressed with a simple run-length encoding to keep artifacts small. Backwards compatibility: tracing is opt-in and defaults to off so production behavior is unchanged. Performance: measured overhead with tracing on is ~2-3% for short sequences and up to 7% during heavy kernel churn; with tracing off overhead is nil. See release notes for more details.

title: Queue-horizon throttling and KV probe backoff for continuous batcher
preview: Motivation: recent production incidents showed pathological tail latency when short-lived high-priority traffic interleaved with KV-heavy long-context requests. The continuous batcher could become congested by probe/burst requests that pin KV slots and prevent fairness tokens from progressing, causing latency spikes. This PR introduces a queue-horizon throttling policy and an exponential backoff for KV probe requests to reduce transient pin contention while preserving throughput.

title: Proportional backfill allocator and soft-reservation smoothing for Dedicated fleets
preview:
Motivation: Dedicated fleets often have fragmented reserved capacity where tenants underutilize their reservations for short windows. Current behavior returns unused tokens aggressively which leads to oscillation when reservations are re-acquired and contributes to noisy autoscaler decisions. This PR implements a proportional backfill allocator plus soft-reservation smoothing to allow unused reserved capacity to be temporarily shared across tenants in a controlled, decaying manner.

What this changes (single string): 1) Proportional backfill allocator: when there is spare reserved headroom, we compute per-tenant proportional backfill based on their active demand and reservation weight. Backfill amounts are distributed as fractional credits with deterministic rounding to avoid starvation. Backfill is tagged with ttl and a reclaimable flag so original owners can reclaim capacity within the reclaim window. 2) Soft-reservation smoothing: when reservations change (scale down/release), we do not return all freed capacity immediately. Instead we put freed tokens into a smoothing pool that decays with configurable half-life (default: 60s). This reduces allocation volatility when tenants frequently adjust reservations or experience microbursts. 3) Autoscaler policy hook: autoscaler now includes a backfill-aware decision step. Scale-down decisions subtract smoothing pool before computing headroom so we avoid premature scale-ups triggered by transient backfill. 4) Telemetry: new metrics added - fleet.backfill.utilization_pct, fleet.backfill.reclaimed_events_total, fleet.backfill.decay_pool_size, tenant.backfill.received_tokens_total. Dashboards: new console dashboard for backfill behavior and a backfill heatmap for the last 24h. 5) Feature flag: opt-in behind config proportional_backfill_v1; default disabled for existing fleets. 6) Tests: unit + integration tests that simulate reservation churn, reclaim races, and fairness checks.

Checklist: - [x] Core allocator + smoothing implementation - [x] Unit tests (edge rounding/TTL) - [x] Integration test covering reclaim races - [x] Metrics emitted and dashboard wiring - [x] Docs for ops and rollout - [x] Feature flag gating and safe defaults - [x] Manual canary run in staging (see rollout notes)

Files changed (high level): allocator core, smoothing state machine, autoscaler hook, metrics registry, console dashboard, docs, feature flag config, tests and CI pipeline additions for integration tests. No public API changes to the Redwood API surface.

Review conversation (summary):
- Liam: raised concern about fairness under extreme skew (90/10) and suggested a cap per-tenant to avoid monopolization. PR added a per-tenant max_backfill_pct and tests.
- Priya: asked for explicit reclaim semantics when original owner scales back up rapidly; I added a reclaim window (default 15s) and deterministic priority for reclaiming older backfill credits first.
- Evan: requested telemetry labels for fleet_id and reservation_type; added those labels and lightweight cardinality guards.
- QA: flagged a timing flake in integration test; fixed by moving from wall-clock sleeps to a simulated tickable clock in tests.
- Security: validated no elevation of privilege since backfill is an allocator layer only and does not change auth.

CI notes: new integration tests increase pipeline time by ~2m. First run had a flaky that was fixed; final CI status is pass.

Rollout plan: gate behind proportional_backfill_v1 feature flag. Start with canary fleets (staging and two low-traffic prod fleets) for 48h, observe fleet.backfill.* metrics and autoscaler scale-up rates. If stable, expand to 20% of dedicated fleets for 7 days, then full rollout. Emergency rollback: flip feature flag off.

Backwards compatibility / risk: opt-in behavior by default. The allocator adds an internal pool but does not change reservation accounting semantics. Risk mode: incorrect smoothing params could increase tail latency for under-reserved tenants; mitigated by conservative default params and telemetry.

Release notes: Introduces a controlled proportional backfill allocator and soft-reservation smoothing for Dedicated fleets (feature-flagged). Adds telemetry and a console dashboard. Opt-in default.

title: introduce-cost-rules-engine-and-autotune-batching-defaults
preview: Summary: Add a Cost Rules Engine that automatically recommends and applies batching and KV-cache defaults per model and workload profile, exposes quantization profile hints for safe auto-application, and introduces a unit-economics estimator in the console and CLI. Motivation: customers routinely ask for hands-off cost tuning; operational teams want reproducible, audited defaults that reduce per-token costs without surprising latency regressions. This PR bundles runtime heuristics, a small rules DSL, console UI plumbing, and an opt-in rollout for auto-quantization. What changed (high level): - new runtime module redwood/serving/cost_rules.py with rules evaluation, telemetry hooks, and safe-apply gates - batcher tuning extension that selects default batch sizes based on latency SLOs and sequence-length histograms - KV cache policy defaults and prefix cache eligibility heuristics - quantization profiling pipeline (offline profiler + runtime hinting) and a safe auto-apply policy with rollback - console/CLI cost estimator showing projected per-token unit economics with suggested knobs - integration tests, docs, and an opt-in feature flag + audit logs Rollout plan: default rules are disabled by default for existing tenants. We enable the rules engine for internal canaries and add a gradual opt-in toggle in the console. Quantization auto-apply is gated behind a policy that requires: a) offline eval against golden prompt set, b) latency/regression guardrails, and c) explicit tenant opt-in. Checklist: - [x] implement rule evaluator and runtime hooks - [x] expose console estimator and API endpoints - [x] add offline quant profile pipeline and safe-apply path - [x] add unit + integration tests - [x] add docs and release notes - [x] add audit events and rollout flag Notes on safety: the rules engine never mutates a serving model binary directly; it returns a recommended configuration which a controlled subsystem applies with canarying and audits. The PR includes a conservative default profile that favors latency SLOs for small-sequence chat workloads and cost for high-throughput embedding workloads. Related: ENG-3412 (cost-automation epic), ENG-3470 (quantization-safety), SUP-1298 (customer support case motivating default cache rules).

title: Add Security FAQ / RFP snippets to Internal Admin (approved-language library + API)
preview: Summary: This PR adds first-class “Security Snippets” support to Internal Admin so we can centrally manage approved FAQ/RFP text blocks (and their versions) that Sales/SE tooling consumes. This is part of the Enterprise Security Enablement Kit refresh: we’ve had repeated drift between Drive/Confluence snippets, HubSpot templates, and ad-hoc copy/paste. The goal is a single source of truth with clear ownership, review metadata, and safe distribution semantics (no restricted evidence in snippets). Motivation / context: Enterprise security reviews are gating Dedicated/Private deals; field teams need fast, consistent answers without over-claiming. Today, approved language lives across Confluence + Drive and gets copied into various tools. This introduces: (1) a canonical snippet store; (2) an API for downstream consumption; (3) RBAC + audit log events for changes; (4) explicit versioning + “last reviewed” metadata to align with enablement kit governance. Scope (what’s included): - New snippet domain model + persistence: `security_snippets` table (Postgres) with: `key` (stable identifier), `title`, `category`, `deployment_modes` (Hosted/Dedicated/Private flags stored as text), `content_markdown`, `status` (draft|in_review|approved|deprecated), `version`, `last_reviewed_at`, `expires_at` (optional), `owner` (team/alias), `approver` (name), and `notes_internal` (e.g., references to evidence request playbook). - Versioning semantics: update creates a new version row (immutable content by version). “Approved” is per-version; deprecating a version does not delete it. - Internal Admin UI: new “Security & Compliance → Snippets” page with: list + filters (category, status, deployment mode), diff viewer between versions, and an “export” view (raw markdown or plaintext) for downstream tools. - Public/consumption API (internal): `GET /internal/snippets/security?status=approved` and `GET /internal/snippets/security/{key}`. The API returns only non-restricted fields; explicitly excludes `notes_internal` and any future evidence references. - Permissions: gated behind Internal Admin RBAC permission `admin.security_snippets.write` (write) and `admin.security_snippets.read` (read). Default: read for Sales Ops + Solutions Eng; write limited to Security/GRC + Product Security PM. (See reviewers’ notes below re: default grants.) - Audit logging: emits `security.snippet.created`, `security.snippet.updated`, `security.snippet.status_changed` with actor, snippet key, version, and timestamp. These show up in existing audit log pipelines for internal systems. - Seeded initial snippet keys: a small curated set to validate workflow (SSO/SAML, SCIM, RBAC granularity, audit log retention/export, data use/no-training language, encryption in transit/at rest, SOC 2 report request steps). The exact text was copied from the working GTM draft library and then reviewed in this PR; it is not intended to be a complete library in this change. Out of scope / explicit non-goals: - No new product features or promises; no roadmap language. - No restricted compliance evidence (SOC 2 report, pen test reports, vuln scan artifacts) is stored here. Snippets may include the approved process for requesting evidence under NDA, but not the evidence itself. - No direct HubSpot/CRM integration in this PR; the first consumer is the generic response tool (tracked separately) and manual use via export. Implementation details: - Backend: `internal-admin-api` adds `SecuritySnippetService` + repository; migrations in `migrations/2026_02_07_security_snippets.sql`. - Frontend: `internal-admin-ui` adds `SecuritySnippetsPage`, `SnippetEditor` (markdown), and `VersionDiffModal`. - API response includes `content_markdown` and `content_text` (server-side stripped markdown) to reduce divergence between tools that support rich text vs plain text. - All endpoints require admin auth; responses include `etag` and support `If-None-Match` to reduce polling load for downstream tooling. Changed areas (high level): - `services/internal-admin-api/src/security_snippets/*` - `services/internal-admin-api/src/routes/internal/snippets_security.ts` - `services/internal-admin-ui/src/pages/security/SecuritySnippetsPage.tsx` - `packages/rbac/policies/internal-admin.yaml` - `packages/audit-events/src/events/security_snippets.ts` - DB migration and seeds under `services/internal-admin-api/db/*` Example snippet (illustrative, shortened): Key: `sso_saml_scim_overview` Title: “SSO (SAML) + SCIM support” Category: “Access Controls” Content (excerpt): “Redwood supports SAML 2.0 SSO for the Redwood Console and supports SCIM 2.0 for user provisioning with supported IdPs. Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced for console actions and API keys. For details on supported IdPs and RBAC roles, see the internal capability reference. If a requirement is not currently supported, follow the Security Questionnaire Exceptions Policy.” Test plan: - Unit tests: service + repository (create, new version on update, status transitions) - API tests: auth required; status filter; ETag behavior - UI smoke test: create draft (GRC/Sec role), request review, approve, view diff - Verified audit events appear in internal audit log stream for a status change - Manual: export approved snippets as plaintext and paste into a sample RFP questionnaire section (formatting preserved reasonably) Security / compliance considerations: - RBAC gating prevents broad write access; all mutations are audited. - “No evidence stored” is enforced by process + UI copy: editor includes a warning banner and blocks uploads/attachments. - PII: none expected; we added a lint check in editor that warns (non-blocking) if content appears to include email addresses or phone numbers. Review conversation (highlights): - Marcus Lin (Security Eng): requested audit event fields include `snippet_key` + `version` and that the update path is versioned (not in-place). Addressed in commit `a8b3f2c` (adds `version` and immutability). Approved after changes. - Olga Petrov (GRC): requested stronger do-not-say guardrails and explicit “evidence request under NDA” language in SOC 2 snippet; also requested that ISO language avoids implying certification. Updated seeded snippets and added an `approved_claims_tags` field (string list stored as comma-separated text) to support future reporting; tags are not yet exposed to consumers. Approved. - Naomi Feldman (Product Security PM): requested that deployment mode filters be explicit (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) and that any roadmap mention be removed from seeded audit logging snippet. Updated copy and added deployment mode UI chips. Approved. - Camila Reyes (Product Marketing): requested consistency with security messaging pillars (terminology: “Hosted API / Dedicated / Private”) and removal of ambiguous “industry standard” claims in the generic encryption snippet. Updated snippet text accordingly. Approved. - Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): requested plaintext export option and that snippets be searchable by common questionnaire phrasing (“Do you train on customer data?”, “SIEM export”, “SCIM”). Added full-text search across title/content and `keywords` field (stored as text). Approved. CI status: - Build: pass - Unit tests: pass - Lint: pass - Migration check: pass - UI build: pass Rollout / ops notes: - Feature is behind existing Internal Admin access; new page visible only with `admin.security_snippets.read`. - Seed data runs in staging only; production will start empty and be populated by Security/GRC after the enablement kit review cycle. - Follow-up: wire the generic response tool to this API and enforce “approved only” in consumers (tracked in INT-1234 + PM-4829 follow-up tasks).

Updated copy and added deployment mode UI chips. Approved. - Camila Reyes (Product Marketing): requested consistency with security messaging pillars (terminology: “Hosted API / Dedicated / Private”) and removal of ambiguous “industry standard” claims in the generic encryption snippet. Updated snippet text accordingly. Approved. - Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): requested plaintext export option and that snippets be searchable by common questionnaire phrasing (“Do you train on customer data?”, “SIEM export”, “SCIM”). Added full-text search across title/content and `keywords` field (stored as text). Approved. title: SDKS: auth mosaic + streaming consolidation and ergonomic API polish
preview: Context: We observed subtle divergence in how Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs handled token refresh during long-lived streams and how retry presets influence stream resume characteristics. This caused two classes of production issues: (1) opaque reauth races that produced 401s during resume, and (2) inconsistent backpressure hints leading to latency spikes when batching heuristics changed across SDKs. This PR introduces a small cross-SDK surface (SessionSurface) and a compact "mosaic" of auth utilities to align behavior. Key goals: harmonize reauth handshake, provide ergonomic retry presets (latency-first and throughput-first), and expose minimal stream backpressure hints to SDK consumers. Changes: - Introduce SessionSurface (py) and SessionFactory (go) as a lightweight facade for long-lived sessions; exported helpers in ts for parity. - Add two retry preset bundles: latency_aware and throughput_aware (shared canonical definitions in pkg/common/retry). - Token choreography: tokenHelper/ensureToken (ts) and a small auth-mosaic helper (py/go) to centralize refresh windows and jitter. - Streaming ergonomics: backpressure hints API (ts) + iterator safety guard (go) + python convenience wrapper for async generators. - Docs & examples: migration guide with concrete recipes for upgrading long-lived streams; example showing how to pick retry presets and combine with SessionSurface. Testing: unit coverage across SDKs; manual smoke runs against a dedicated canary stack. CI: one flaky legacy test in sdks/python was rerun and passed on the second run. Checklist: - [x] API design reviews with SDK leads - [x] Cross-language parity tests - [x] Changelog entries and migration guide - [x] Version bumps for SDK packages (py 1.13.0, ts 1.29.0, go 1.12.2) Rollout plan: merge to main, cut coordinated SDK releases; library consumers that rely on automatic token refresh should switch to the new SessionSurface/Factory or call the token helper from their stream bootstrap code. Related: ENG-2345 (stream reliability), PM-7890 (developer ergonomics).

title: Console: Shadow experiment results page (quality deltas + example drilldowns) behind feature flag
preview:
## Summary
Adds the Console UI for viewing Shadow Experiment results: quality deltas vs control, confidence intervals, segment breakdowns, and a drilldown drawer for scored examples.

This is explicitly *read-only* UI for the beta. Creation flow remains in the existing experiment wizard (DES-611 work), and the results page is linked from Rollouts timeline + the Shadow Experiments list.

Everything in this PR is gated behind `shadow_experiments_results_ui` (per tenant). When disabled, routes fall back to the existing placeholder/"Coming soon" page.

## Why
We have shadow traffic shipping in dogfood and early Dedicated beta, but teams can’t validate quality changes without an at-a-glance view of deltas and a way to inspect examples. This PR wires up the results UX to the new aggregates + examples endpoints from the Console backend (PR #24933) and renders:
- Quality delta charts (candidate vs control), with 95% CI bands and significance callout when applicable
- Segment filters (safe dimensions only) + time range picker
- "Top regressions" table and per-metric deep links
- Example drilldown drawer (prompt/response *redacted*) with rubric breakdowns and scorer metadata

## Feature flag
- Flag: `shadow_experiments_results_ui`
- Default: off
- Enabled for: internal dogfood tenant only (handled via INT-912 / config rollout)

## UX highlights
- Results header: Experiment name, route, control target, shadow target(s), sampling %, rubric version, data retention indicator
- Metric cards: e.g. `format_adherence_rate`, `refusal_rate`, `instruction_following_score_mean`, `safety_policy_violation_rate`
- Chart behavior:
  - Shows control and candidate series + delta band view toggle
  - Confidence band computed server-side; UI simply renders (no client stats)
  - Tooltips show sample size (n), window, and whether samples are shadow-sampled vs prompt-set
- Segment filters (cardinality-safe): `region`, `request_type`, `model_variant`, `status_class` (2xx/4xx/5xx), `token_bucket` (bucketed)
  - Explicitly *not* supported in UI: `prompt_id`, `customer_id`, raw `route_id` free text (route is chosen from context)

## API wiring
- GET `/console/v1/shadow-experiments/:id/aggregates?window=1h&range=24h&segment=region`
- GET `/console/v1/shadow-experiments/:id/examples?metric=format_adherence_rate&outcome=regression&limit=50&cursor=...`
- Error/empty states:
  - If scoring lag or no data: show "Waiting for scoring" with link to troubleshooting docs (internal)
  - If RBAC denies `shadow:read_examples`: hide examples tab and show callout

## Implementation details
- Adds `ShadowExperimentResultsPage` route + navigation entry under Observability → Shadow Experiments
- New components:
  - `QualityDeltaChart` (line + band)
  - `MetricDeltaCard` (delta, CI, sample size)
  - `SegmentFilterBar` (safe enum dims only)
  - `ScoredExampleDrawer` (prompt/response + rubric breakdown)
- Uses existing design system primitives (Panels, Callouts, Tables, Skeleton loaders)
- Maintains strict PII posture:
  - UI never renders raw prompt/response unless backend returns redacted text (expected)
  - Displays explicit "Redacted" markers when fields omitted

## Tests
- Unit tests:
  - chart toggle behavior (series vs delta)
  - segment filter param mapping + resets
  - flag gating renders placeholder when off
- Playwright:
  - loads results page, applies time range + segment, opens example drawer

## Screenshots (internal)
- Results overview: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-overview
- Segment breakdown: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-segments
- Example drawer: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-example

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable for internal tenant only (ENG-5416)
3) Enable for 2 Dedicated beta customers after SRE signoff on scoring lag dashboards (ENG-5409)

## Checklist
- [x] Feature flag gating added
- [x] RBAC checked before showing example drilldowns
- [x] Empty/scoring-lag states
- [x] Segment dimensions constrained to allowlist
- [x] Basic Playwright coverage

## Related
- Backend APIs: PR #24933
- Dashboards: observability-pack PR #391
- ADRs: adr-shadow-metrics-data-model-and-cardinality, quality-metrics-observability-standard

---

### Commit summary
1. console: add shadow experiment results route + nav entry
2. console: implement quality delta chart + metric cards
3. console: add segment filter bar with dimension allowlist
4. console: add scored examples table + drawer (RBAC gated)
5. console: wire up API client + pagination + retries
6. console: add tests (unit + Playwright)

### Changed areas (high level)
- `console/apps/web/src/routes/shadowExperiments/ShadowExperimentResultsPage.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/components/quality/QualityDeltaChart.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/components/quality/ScoredExampleDrawer.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/api/shadowExperiments.ts`
- `console/apps/web/src/flags/featureFlags.ts`
- `console/apps/web/tests/playwright/shadowExperimentsResults.spec.ts`

Small illustrative snippet (flag gating):

if (!flags.shadow_experiments_results_ui) {
  return <ShadowExperimentsResultsPlaceholder />;
}

### CI
- web:lint ✅
- web:typecheck ✅
- web:test ✅
- web:playwright ✅ (reran once due to flake; see conversation)

### Review conversation (highlights)
Kenji Nakamura (requested changes): "Chart density is a bit high; can we default to 24h and keep 7d behind a secondary selector? Also the CI band needs a lighter tint to match the rest of the Console charts."

Dylan Brooks (author): "Updated defaults to 24h and moved 7d/30d into the time-range dropdown. Adjusted band opacity to 12% and used the `--chart-band` token. Pushed in 5f7c3b1."

Allison Grant: "Segment filter list looks safe but please hard-block arbitrary querystring dims; we’ve been burned by people copy/pasting links with `segment=prompt_id`."

Dylan Brooks: "Added allowlist validation + fallback to `none` with a toast. Also added a small unit test for invalid segment values."

Sean Gallagher: "Make sure example drawer doesn’t fetch examples automatically on page load; this can spike load when people share links."

Dylan Brooks: "Good call — examples now fetch only when the Examples tab is opened, and we cap initial load at 25 with explicit pagination."

Chloe Martin: "API client should pass `rubric_version` through for display, but don’t use it to branch UI logic yet."

Dylan Brooks: "Wired `rubric_version` into header display only. No conditional rendering."

Marissa Cole: "Can we add a callout making it clear this doesn’t affect user traffic and is asynchronous?" 

Dylan Brooks: "Added info callout under header: 'Shadow results are computed asynchronously and may lag by up to N minutes.' (N pulled from backend config)."

CI note: Playwright failed once with a timeout on opening the example drawer in staging; reran and passed. Added an extra wait on the skeleton -> content transition.

### Merge
Merged via squash into `main`.

title: Audit logs for shadow experiment + shadow policy changes
preview:
Summary: Adds audit log entries for shadow experiment lifecycle actions (create/update/archive) and for route-attached shadow policy updates. This is part of Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics beta readiness: we need a clear who/what/when trail for experiment changes and policy toggles, especially for Dedicated/Private tenants with compliance requirements.

Context / Motivation: Shadow traffic is explicitly “fail-open” and non-user-impacting, but misconfiguration can still create meaningful cost/retention risk (sampling rate, targets, redaction flags). During dogfood and early customer discussions, we’ve been asked to provide auditable records for: (1) who created/changed an experiment, (2) who attached/detached shadow policies to routes/rollouts, and (3) what fields changed. This PR extends the existing AuditLog service to capture the relevant actions and adds minimal schema support for structured diffs.

What’s included: (1) New audit event types: SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_CREATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_ARCHIVED, SHADOW_POLICY_ATTACHED, SHADOW_POLICY_UPDATED, SHADOW_POLICY_DETACHED. (2) Console backend wiring: emits audit events from ShadowExperiments CRUD endpoints and from policy mutation endpoints used by rollouts. (3) Schema updates: adds resource_type = "shadow_experiment" | "shadow_policy" and resource_id fields, plus optional change_summary (field-level diff) with allowlisted keys to avoid leaking prompt/content. (4) RBAC + attribution: ensures actor is the authenticated user/service principal; includes org_id + project_id + environment in the audit payload for correlation in Private deployments. (5) Tests: unit tests for event emission and redaction/allowlist behavior; integration test verifies a mutation request produces a single audit row with stable event_type.

Not included / Follow-ups: No UI changes in Console for viewing these events (tracked separately). No backfill for historic changes prior to this PR. We intentionally do not log prompt content, model outputs, or header values; only configuration metadata and allowlisted field diffs.

Implementation notes: Audit payload fields are explicitly allowlisted. For SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, we capture changes for: name, description, sampling_rate, target_variants, rubric_bundle_id, and retention_mode. For shadow policy events, we capture: enabled, sampling_rate, shadow_targets (IDs only), fail_open, and timeout_ms. Anything else is recorded as "[redacted]" in change_summary.

Risk: Low. Audit emission is best-effort and does not block the write path (fail-open). If AuditLog is down/slow, we enqueue to the existing async audit buffer with bounded retries.

Validation: Ran console-backend unit tests + integration suite, and verified in staging that create/update/archive actions produce expected event types with correct resource_id. Confirmed no request body or prompt fields end up in the audit record.

Screenshots/Logs: Included in internal staging run (see test output in CI artifacts).

Release note (internal): Shadow experiments and shadow policies now generate audit log events on create/update/archive/attach/detach.

title: Shadow traffic: telemetry events + ingestion hooks (v1)
preview:
Context: Shadow traffic routing (ENG-5402) mirrors a sampled subset of prod requests to one or more shadow targets. To compute quality deltas (and to debug routing/scoring lag) we need a low-cardinality, privacy-aware set of telemetry events that (a) links the control and shadow inference outcomes, (b) can be ingested reliably in Hosted/Dedicated/Private, and (c) provides enough dimensions to slice by route/model/region without exploding metrics cardinality.

This PR introduces shadow traffic telemetry events and wires them into the ingestion pipeline (Kafka -> ingest -> warehouse tables) with initial dashboard stubs for internal dogfood.

What’s included: (1) New event schemas: shadow_pair_created (emitted when the mirror is scheduled), shadow_inference_completed (shadow target returned or timed out), and shadow_scoring_status (async scoring queued/completed/failed). Each event includes a stable shadow_pair_id and request_fingerprint (HMAC), plus required dimensions: tenant_id (hashed), route_id (low-card), control_model_id, shadow_target_id, deployment_mode, region, outcome, and timing fields. (2) Cardinality controls: disallow freeform tags in shadow telemetry, bucket user_agent, truncate prompt_length/response_length into fixed buckets, and explicitly drop prompt_id / conversation_id at emit-time. Route-level and model-level dimensions are included; anything higher-card must be attached via example sampling endpoints later (out of scope). (3) Ingestion pipeline hooks: adds validators/normalizers for the new events, forwards them through the existing ingest service, and creates the initial storage tables shadow_pairs_v1 and shadow_scoring_v1 (partitioned by day, clustered by tenant_hash + route_id). (4) Initial dashboards stubs: adds basic Grafana JSON stubs for shadow throughput, shadow target error rate, and scoring lag; these are intentionally minimal and will be expanded in observability-pack PR-391.

PII / data handling: This PR does not store raw prompts/responses. request_fingerprint is computed using an HMAC keyed per environment (see config), and tenant_id is hashed before leaving the data plane. Any headers included are allowlisted (content-type, accept, x-redwood-route) and explicitly exclude auth/cookie-like fields.

Testing: Unit tests for schema validation and normalization, golden tests for event-to-table mapping, and an end-to-end ingest test that emits a synthetic shadow_pair_created + shadow_inference_completed and asserts the warehouse row shape. Load testing / overhead benchmarking is tracked in ENG-5412.

Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag shadow_telemetry_v1 (default off). Dogfood tenants can enable via internal config. Fail-open: telemetry emission failures do not impact request path; ingestion lag only affects dashboards/metrics.

Follow-ups: (a) scoring payload references once redaction pipeline lands (ENG-5411), (b) additional aggregation views for confidence intervals, (c) tighten SLO alert thresholds once we have baseline dogfood data.

Notes for reviewers: please focus on (1) schema field choices and defaults, (2) cardinality guardrails, and (3) whether the ingestion mapping matches the query patterns we expect for Console drilldowns.

This PR introduces shadow traffic telemetry events and wires them into the ingestion pipeline (Kafka -> ingest -> warehouse tables) with initial dashboard stubs for internal dogfood.

What’s included: (1) New event schemas: shadow_pair_created (emitted when the mirror is scheduled), shadow_inference_completed (shadow target returned or timed out), and shadow_scoring_status (async scoring queued/completed/failed). Each event includes a stable shadow_pair_id and request_fingerprint (HMAC), plus required dimensions: tenant_id (hashed), route_id (low-card), control_model_id, shadow_target_id, deployment_mode, region, outcome, and timing fields. (2) Cardinality controls: disallow freeform tags in shadow telemetry, bucket user_agent, truncate prompt_length/response_length into fixed buckets, and explicitly drop prompt_id / conversation_id at emit-time. Route-level and model-level dimensions are included; anything higher-card must be attached via example sampling endpoints later (out of scope). (3) Ingestion pipeline hooks: adds validators/normalizers for the new events, forwards them through the existing ingest service, and creates the initial storage tables shadow_pairs_v1 and shadow_scoring_v1 (partitioned by day, clustered by tenant_hash + route_id). (4) Initial dashboards stubs: adds basic Grafana JSON stubs for shadow throughput, shadow target error rate, and scoring lag; these are intentionally minimal and will be expanded in observability-pack PR-391.

PII / data handling: This PR does not store raw prompts/responses. request_fingerprint is computed using an HMAC keyed per environment (see config), and tenant_id is hashed before leaving the data plane. Any headers included are allowlisted (content-type, accept, x-redwood-route) and explicitly exclude auth/cookie-like fields.

Testing: Unit tests for schema validation and normalization, golden tests for event-to-table mapping, and an end-to-end ingest test that emits a synthetic shadow_pair_created + shadow_inference_completed and asserts the warehouse row shape. Load testing / overhead benchmarking is tracked in ENG-5412.

Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag shadow_telemetry_v1 (default off). Dogfood tenants can enable via internal config. Fail-open: telemetry emission failures do not impact request path; ingestion lag only affects dashboards/metrics.

Follow-ups: (a) scoring payload references once redaction pipeline lands (ENG-5411), (b) additional aggregation views for confidence intervals, (c) tighten SLO alert thresholds once we have baseline dogfood data.

Notes for reviewers: please focus on (1) schema field choices and defaults, (2) cardinality guardrails, and (3) whether the ingestion mapping matches the query patterns we expect for Console drilldowns.

title: Add tier/deployment_mode tags to SLI metrics at ingress (and propagate through request context)
preview: Context: As part of the Tiered SLO + Error Budget program, we need to compute SLIs (success rate, latency, streaming reliability) separately for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity). Today most gateway metrics are emitted without a reliable tier signal, and routing/fallback can further obscure the original customer tier in downstream services. This PR adds tier/deployment mode tags to ingress SLI metrics and ensures the tags are attached to the request context so they can be propagated into routing, traces, and downstream metrics. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a canonical tier classifier at ingress that maps requests into tier={hosted|dedicated} and deployment_mode={hosted_api|dedicated_pool} (naming aligned with ADR-0142 draft). (2) Add these tags to the gateway SLI metric set for request count, request duration histograms, and streaming termination counters. (3) Propagate tier/deployment_mode via RequestContext (headers -> internal ctx) so routing can preserve tier across fallback/region-shift decisions and telemetry can emit consistent tags. (4) Add guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions: we do NOT add customer_id/model_id labels at ingress; those remain sampled/derived in dedicated dashboards only, behind existing access controls. (5) Provide migration notes for dashboards/recording rules: existing queries using legacy label "pool" or "account_tier" should switch to tier/deployment_mode; a compatibility recording rule is included for a 2-week deprecation window. Details: Metrics affected include api_gateway_request_total, api_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket, api_gateway_stream_termination_total, and api_gateway_errors_total. For streaming, we tag the termination reason classification at the gateway boundary (client_abort vs server_disconnect vs upstream_error) using the existing SSE/WebSocket close signals and a small set of heuristics; only server_disconnect and upstream_error count against streaming reliability SLI (client_abort excluded per the SLI spec). Rollout plan: This ships behind config flag telemetry.tier_tagging.enabled (default off). We’ll enable in staging for 48h, then canary 5% prod, then 100% per-region (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1) once dashboards show stable label coverage. After rollout, we will remove the compatibility recording rule and update remaining dashboards/alerts in follow-up PRs. Operational notes: If tier tagging is missing, the request is tagged tier=unknown and deployment_mode=unknown; alerts/dashboards should exclude unknown from SLO accounting (see migration note). This PR intentionally does not change SLO math; it only makes the tier dimension available and consistently propagated.
