title: Packed INT8 microkernel rewrite and token coalescer to improve throughput for small-batch inference
preview:
Context and motivation: small-batch interactive workloads (chat, per-request short-context generation) show poor INT8 utilization because microkernel packing and per-token scheduling produce many narrow GEMM shapes and high per-call overhead. Prior batching heuristics mitigated this at the request layer but left inefficiencies inside the kernel and scheduling layer. This PR introduces two coordinated changes to address that: (1) a packed INT8 microkernel optimized for narrow micro-tiles and reduced memory traffic, and (2) a runtime token coalescer that merges short token sequences across concurrent requests into aligned micro-batches at the kernel boundary.

Design summary:
- Packed INT8 microkernel: new micro-tiling layout (packed 4x8 major order) that reduces memory stride and enables vector-friendly inner loops; fused requantization step merged into the GEMM write-back to avoid a full dequant/q-requant pass for common patterns; safe FP32/FP8 fallback path for layers where requant scales exceed per-tensor range.
- Token coalescer scheduler: lightweight aggregator in the continuous batching pipeline that groups tokens by model variant and sequence length bucket, enforcing alignment to the microkernel tile size. Coalescer is conservative: it only aggregates tokens within configurable latency budget and respects per-tenant isolation.
- Runtime controls and telemetry: feature gates runtime.packed_int8.enabled and runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms; new metrics token_coalescer.groups, microkernel_tile_util, packed_int8_path_ratio.

Commits (high-level):
- feat(kernels): add packed int8 microkernel entrypoints and assembly fallback (3 commits)
- perf(scheduler): token coalescer hook into continuous batching loop (2 commits)
- test: unit + fuzz parity tests for INT8 path (2 commits)
- bench: add small-batch harness + CI microbench (1 commit)
- docs: runtime flags and rollout notes (1 commit)

Files touched (high level):
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.cc
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.h
- asm/packed_int8_asm.S (platform-guarded implementations)
- src/runtime/scheduler/token_coalescer.cc
- src/runtime/scheduler/continuous_batching_integration.cc
- tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc
- benchmarks/bench_small_batch_packed_int8.py
- docs/runtime/flags.md
- examples/packed_int8_quickstart.md
- ci/microbench.yml
- build/BUILD.bazel (kernel entries)
- scripts/perf/compare_a100_t4.sh

Implementation notes:
- Microkernel implements a fused write-back that applies the per-channel scale and zero point during the GEMM output path; this removed one intermediate tensor allocation in the hot loop.
- We chose a 4x8 tile because it balances register pressure and memory coalescing on both A100 and T4; hand-tuned inner loops for AVX3 and CUDA mma-style intrinsics where available.
- Token coalescer hooks before the KV-cache dispatch: it accumulates tokens by (model_id, variant, seq_bucket) and emits coalesced batches up to the microkernel tile alignment or latency budget.

Correctness and parity:
- Added deterministic parity tests that compare outputs vs FP32 reference on representative micro-batches and randomized seeds.
- FP8 fallback uses an extra path that quantizes to FP8 when per-tensor scale fits; otherwise falls back to per-channel INT8 with a narrow requant path to preserve numerical fidelity.

Benchmarks (representative, run on on-prem clusters):
- A100 (8x latency-bound small queries): baseline INT8 path throughput 1250 tokens/s -> packed-int8 + coalescer 1650 tokens/s (+32%).
- T4 (mixed small-batch): baseline 740 -> 915 tokens/s (+23%).
- Median end-to-end p95 latency change: -4% (slightly improved due to fewer memory copies).
- Token-coalescer shows larger wins for <8-token requests; negligible impact for large batches.

Review conversation (selected excerpts, chronological):
- Ethan Park (review): "Can you add a microbench in CI that asserts no regression in token-level throughput on T4? Also needs a non-regression accuracy test (FP32 vs INT8) for 2M random tokens."
- Author (Monica): "Added microbench.yml and parity test; microbench is gated on an opt-in perf job to avoid CI costs. See tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc."
- Sofia Ruiz (review): "Concern: coalescer may increase tail latency for single-tenant extremes. We should expose latency_ms threshold and default to 5ms; also add tenant opt-out."
- Author: "Exposed runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms with default 5, and runtime.token_coalescer.enable_per_tenant=false; added docs. Token coalescer will never coalesce across tenant boundaries by default."
- Jasper Li (review): "Assembly fallback looks good, but please add a safety check for AVX availability and a clean fallback to scalar path."
- Author: "Implemented CPU capability checks and unit tests for the scalar fallback path."
- CI pipeline: initial run failed due to clang-format on packed_int8_kernel.h and a flaky perf test; lint fixes and re-run produced green CI: unit tests 245/245 passed; microbench opt-in run showed throughput numbers above."

Rollout and rollback plan:
- Feature gates available via runtime flags; rollout to 10% of hosted traffic for first week with telemetry checks.
- Automated regression alerts on model quality (per-prompt edit distance) and token-level checksum parity will trigger immediate rollback.
- If severe regressions occur, we can disable runtime.packed_int8.enabled remotely and revert scheduler hook.

Risk assessment:
- Numerical drift: parity tests and FP8 fallback minimize risk; however, rare edge cases may see small token-level differences—these are surfaced to evals automatically.
- Multi-tenant fairness: coalescer respects tenant isolation; added opt-out flag for customers that prefer per-request isolation.

Follow-ups and TODO:
- Explore widening tile shapes for large-batch paths and auto-tune tile size per-GPU.
- Add cooperative scheduling with dedicated capacity autoscaler to avoid starvation when coalescer groups many small requests.
- Port microkernel to the Windows CUDA toolchain (currently Linux-only guarded).

Merge notes:
- PR was squashed on merge.
- Merged by Ethan Park after approvals and green CI.
- Associated Linear cards closed: ENG-8421, ENG-8484.
- Release: rollout will be gated and documented under v0.67 runtime release notes.

title: Drift‑resilient collective scheduler and KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention
preview:
Context and motivation: In production we continue to see sporadic tail latency spikes on multi‑GPU TP workloads correlated with short windows of NCCL congestion and many small KV cache allocations from continuous batching. This PR introduces two coordinated runtime changes: (1) a drift‑resilient collective scheduler that perturbs and staggers NCCL launches to avoid pathological synchronization across tensor slices, and (2) a KV slab consolidation pass that reduces small allocation churn and improves L2/L3 locality for the KV cache allocator. The combined change targets multi‑GPU tensor‑parallel inference paths (TP runtime) and the continuous‑batching code path used by the API router.

What this PR does (high level): - Adds Scheduler::PerturbationWindow, a lightweight jittering layer that applies per‑peer launch offsets within a configurable window to break lockstep NCCL launches while preserving ordering and progress guarantees. The scheduler also tracks per‑peer outstanding credits and backs off launches when cumulative outstanding bytes exceed a runtime threshold. - Adds KVSlab::Consolidate and a consolidation task that merges hot small slabs into larger contiguous slabs at low priority, reducing per‑token allocation overhead and fragmentation. Consolidation is gated by memory pressure and configurable thresholds so it won’t interfere with SLOs. - Adds a small admission tweak to continuous batching: older requests receive a priority boost in the batch selector to reduce head‑of‑line latency under high QPS. - Benchmarks and regression tests: microbenchmarks for NCCL launch variance, end‑to‑end tp inference perf harness, and an integration test that exercises slab consolidation under load.

Design notes and rationale: - We intentionally avoid deterministic reordering. The perturbation window is randomized per launch but bounded; this prevents pathological worst‑case alignments while keeping collectives efficient. - Consolidation is incremental and infrequent; it uses a background worker with a budgeted runtime slice to avoid interfering with token service. - The NCCL perturbation is conservative: on rigs where we observe no contention, the perturbation window defaults to zero. Operators can tune the window via runtime flags and per‑cluster policies. - The combined effect reduces contention-caused retries and kernel stalls while also lowering memory allocation overhead that exacerbates NVLink traffic.

Commits (summary): - 1a2f3b4: Introduce perturbation scheduler hooks and config knobs; add unit tests for scheduling invariants. - 2b3c4d5: Implement KV slab consolidation pass, background worker, and new allocator stats. - 3c4d5e6: Integrate with continuous batching admission, add perf test harness and perf metrics, update docs and changelog.

Files changed (high level): - runtime/scheduler/perturbation_scheduler.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/kv_slab_consolidator.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/allocator_stats.{cc,h} - runtime/batching/batch_selector.{cc} (priority tweak) - tests/perf/tp_nccl_stress_test.py - ci/perf/benchmarks/launch_variance.yaml - docs/release_notes/2026-02-03-kv-nccl-optimization.md - examples/perf/tuning/README.md

Tiny illustrative snippet (informational only): "Scheduler::PerturbationWindow" accepts a window_ns and seed; when >0 it computes offset = rng.uniform(0, window_ns) and schedules NCCL launch at planned_time + offset. KVSlab::Consolidate scans slab list for contiguous small slabs with same owner and merges them under lock.

Review conversation (highlights): - Ethan Park (reviewer): "Nice isolation of perturbation logic. A few requests: make the default window configurable via env var, and add a per‑node debug metric for perturbed launches." Author reply: "Added RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS env and metric runtime.scheduler.perturbed_launches_count; updated tests." - Lucia Ramirez: "Concern about consolidation causing a stop‑the‑world when merging many small slabs." Author reply: "Consolidation uses a background budgeted worker; added unit test and a runtime flag to disable consolidation during low latency SLO periods." - Owen Patel: "Can we get a perf comparison on a 8xA100 TP run?" Author reply: "Included 8x A100 numbers below; will add CI perf job once this merges." - Followups: minor doc edits, one flaky test disabled and a TODO added to re-enable after allocator lock refactor.

CI summary: - Linux unit tests: pass - Integration perf harness (short): pass - Lint: pass - Extended perf run (internal): nominal but required one rerun due to infra transient. Overall status: pass.

Benchmarks (selected results, end‑to‑end TP inference harness, 8x A100, mixed sequence lengths): - Baseline (main): median throughput = 1000 infer/s, p99 latency = 210ms, peak NVLink utilization = 78% - This PR: median throughput = 1140 infer/s (+14%), p99 latency = 185ms (-11.9%), peak NVLink utilization = 66% (-15.4%) - KV memory fragmentation (tracked via allocator_stats.hot_slab_frag): baseline 0.18, this PR 0.09 (-50%) Notes: numbers averaged across 20 runs with warm‑up. Workloads with many small prefixes saw larger gains.

Backward compatibility and operator impact: - Defaults keep behavior unchanged (window defaults to 0, consolidation disabled under heavy SLO by default). - Operators can enable the window and consolidation via flags or cluster policy in the console. - Added new runtime metrics and two flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS and RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED documented in docs/release_notes.

Follow‑ups and TODOs: - Add an operator dashboard tile to surface perturbed launch counts and consolidation activity. - Extend consolidation heuristics to account for pinned memory slabs. - Add a canary rollout in Dedicated environments to validate across different network topologies.

Release notes (short): "Introduce a drift‑resilient collective scheduler and background KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention and small allocation overhead; typical throughput gains 10–15% on multi‑GPU TP workloads and reduced p99 latency. Flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS, RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED."

Related: ENG-1284 (collective tail latency task), ENG-1301 (kv allocator fragmentation).

title: Deprecate legacy stream shims; add credential lookup facade and unify SDK iterables
preview: Context: Multiple SDK consumers reported subtle differences in auth lookup timing and streaming iterator shapes across languages. The legacy 'stream shims' layer introduced in earlier rollouts has become a maintenance burden and makes cross-language parity work harder. Motivation: Reduce duplicated auth lookup code, surface a single lightweight facade for credential resolution that all SDKs call into, and simplify streaming APIs to a single async iterator/pull-generator mapping with clearer backpressure hints. What this PR does: 1) Adds a Credential Lookup Facade (sdk/common/cred_lookup) that centralizes token resolution, TTL jitter logic, and prefetch hints used by the Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs. 2) Reworks streaming surface so each SDK exports a single ergonomic async/pull iterator that maps to the runtime streaming protocol; legacy shims (StreamShimV1) are deprecated and left as compatibility wrappers that call the new facade. 3) Adds a small migration compatibility layer for applications that relied on StreamShimV1 side effects; the layer emits deprecation warnings and forwards calls. 4) Harmonizes default retry profiles for auth failures when streaming (fast-fail then background prefetch) and documents new default presets. 5) Bumps SDK versions and updates changelogs and examples in the quickstarts repo. Testing and validation: unit tests across all three SDKs, end-to-end smoke tests using the live test matrix, and cross-language parity test harness; fixed two flaky tests related to heartbeat timing. Rollout plan: merged to main and will be published behind feature flags for hosted customers for two weeks, then SDK releases will flip the default. Migration notes: existing apps continue to work with the provided shim; consumers should switch to the new iterator surface and call cred_lookup.lookup(tokenHints) for advanced prefetch semantics. Backwards compatibility window: one release cycle for the shim; plan to remove StreamShimV1 in three months with a dedicated deprecation announcement.

title: Coordinated credential handoff, compact retry presets, and generator stream ergonomics across SDKs
preview:
Motivation: We keep getting support tickets and cross-SDK bugs around: (1) credential handoff semantics when a streaming connection needs to rotate/refresh tokens mid-session, (2) divergent ergonomic surfaces for generator/async-iterator based streaming in each SDK, and (3) inconsistent retry presets that lead to surprising latency/cost tradeoffs in low-latency apps. This PR centralizes the contract in the monorepo and ships coordinated changes for the TypeScript/Python/Go SDKs and runtime float so customers can opt into a single predictable behaviour.

What this PR does (high-level): Add a CredentialHandoff contract (lightweight interface + reference implementation), introduce a new compact retry preset set (compact-1/2/3) tuned for short-sequence, latency-sensitive workloads, and unify the public generator/async-iterator surface across SDKs so streaming ergonomics are consistent and migration paths are obvious. This branch contains three smaller commits for the mono-repo: (A) contract + docs + tests, (B) SDK adapters and examples in ts/py/go packages, (C) version bumps and release notes.

Compatibility story: The default behaviour is backward compatible. SDKs expose opt-in flags: `credentialHandoff: 'auto' | 'explicit'` and `retryPreset: 'standard' | 'compact-2'`. Existing consumers continue to operate without change. The generator surface adds new helpers (e.g. `streamToAsyncGenerator`) that are additive wrappers; we also include a short migration note in the changelog.

Testing & verification: unit tests added for the handoff state machine, e2e integration test that simulates token expiry during a 90s long-stream request (CI runs a mocked auth server), and cross-SDK interop smoke tests that run TS -> runtime -> Go client scenarios. Local perf bench indicates compact-3 reduces median per-token latency by ~8% on short sequences due to reduced retry delays and more aggressive early-fails for known 4xx cases.

Follow-ups: coordinate with infra to roll kernel backoff tuning (ENG-2110), and schedule a docs rollout with the Developer Experience team to publish migration examples and a short cookbook (ENG-2199).

title: Holdpoint-carousel phased promote with rehearsal-shadow replay and rollback playbook
preview:
Summary: Introduces a holdpoint-driven 'carousel' promote strategy aimed at safe staged promotes across session-sensitive workloads. The feature combines (1) holdpoint anchors — deterministic traffic cut points keyed by session cohorts, (2) a rehearsal shadow runner that replays representative traffic for each holdpoint prior to live promotion, and (3) a rollback playbook that encodes automatic and manual abort semantics with quarantine lanes.

Motivation: Multiple incidents where incremental canaries passed aggregate metrics but failed for sticky-session cohorts motivated a promotion strategy that ensures session-coherent rehearsals before traffic cutover. This PR implements the orchestration primitives and a default rollback playbook that operators can tune.

Design:
- Holdpoint controller: computes holdpoints from cohort selectors and session affinity metadata, maintains per-holdpoint state and progress checkpoints.
- Rehearsal shadow runner: lightweight traffic replayer that runs sampled requests against candidate model variants with KV-cache warmup and smoke checks.
- Rollback playbook: policy-as-data YAML-driven playbook mapping failure classes (latency spike, token-error regression, model drift) to actions (quarantine, immediate revert, partial drain, human-latch).
- Integration with Console: exposes per-holdpoint timelines, rehearsal badges, and playbook-executed actions.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement controller state machine and progress checkpoints
- [x] Add rehearsal runner with deterministic replay seed
- [x] Provide default rollback playbook and validation harness
- [x] Unit tests for controller transitions
- [x] Integration tests (simulated sessions)
- [x] Console telemetry and holdpoint UI hooks
- [x] Docs and quickstart snippet

Backwards compatibility: The new promote strategy is opt-in via rollout_profile: "holdpoint-carousel". Existing rollout profiles are unchanged. Config changes are additive and safe for clusters that do not opt-in.

Testing:
- Local unit test suites under test/unit or test/orchestrator run with new mock session registry.
- Integration harness has a new scenario that simulates 10k session handoffs across 3 holdpoints and validates session affinity + KV prewarm.
- Ran canary smoke on staging with 2 rehearsal runs; CI flake fixed by increasing test harness timeouts.

Rollout plan: Stage to staging for 48h, enable feature flag for 5% of traffic (non-critical customers), run two rehearsal cycles and collect per-holdpoint smoke metrics, then raise to 20% with automatic rollback monitoring on the playbook thresholds.

Related: builds on ENG-4321 (session-affine routing) and follows the telemetry contract in ENG-4399.

Files changed (high level):
- src/orchestrator/holdpoint_controller.py: new state machine and API hooks
- src/orchestrator/rehearsal_runner.go: sample-driven replay engine with KV warmup
- src/orchestrator/playbooks/rollback_playbook.yaml: policy-as-data examples
- console/ui/holdpoint_timeline.tsx: UI badges and timeline
- docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md: design + quickstart
- test/integration/holdpoint_carousel_test.go: integration scenario
- cfg/rollout_profiles/defaults.yaml: new profile entry

Operational concerns & mitigation:
- Cost: rehearsal runs increase token spend; playbook includes a rehearsal_budget parameter and sampling controls.
- Latency: KV warmup performed in rehearsal; live cutover waits for warmup checkpoint.
- Observability: added per-holdpoint smoke histograms and replay diffs; console links to sample traces.

Rollback semantics: the playbook exposes three abort modes: fast-revert (squash), partial-drain (graceful rollback of subsequent holdpoints), and quarantine (isolate traffic, escalate to human-latch). See rollback_playbook.yaml for mappings and default thresholds.

Documentation: A new page docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md includes conceptual diagrams, playbook examples, and a quickstart.

Migration notes: none required for existing profiles; operators must opt-in to use the new profile.

Open items:
- Add CLI tooling to generate rehearsal seeds from production trace (follow-up PR)
- Add deterministic trace replay for embeddings-heavy workloads (research task)

title: introduce-stream-sanitizer-window-and-functioncall-flush-to-preserve-json-span-contiguity
preview: Motivation: Intermittent production incidents and fuzz tests revealed cases where SSE chunk boundaries could split JSON object spans or function-call sentinel tokens in ways that make downstream parsers emit truncated or mis-ordered events. This caused structured-output consumers and OpenAI-compatibility wrappers to drop function-call arguments or mis-associate tokens when partial frames were replayed. This PR introduces a lightweight streaming sanitizer layer and a small reflow window that: 1) keeps a minimal lookback buffer for boundary repair, 2) normalizes delimiter emission order for function/tool-call sentinels, and 3) applies a safe escape-rewrite for split UTF-8/JSON token fragments. The description below summarizes changes, risks, testing, and rollout plan. Changes made: - runtime/sse/stream_window.go: new StreamSanitizer that maintains a sliding reflow window (configurable max bytes, default 4KB) and detects partial JSON spans using a fast heuristic (brace/quote counters + UTF-8 continuation checks). When a span crosses the window boundary the sanitizer defers emit and attempts repair by joining adjacent chunks or emitting a rescue-marker event. - runtime/sse/protocol.go: added deterministic ordering guarantees for sentinel emissions (heartbeat -> preamble -> function_call_start -> payload chunks -> function_call_end). This avoids reordered sentinel interleaving under high-concurrency flush. - openai/compat/adapter.py: adapter updated to understand the new rescue-marker event and to tolerate delayed function_call_end tokens; compatibility shim will buffer until a matching end or rescue marker. - runtime/kv/cache_adapter.rs: minor change to avoid flushing cached prefixes in the middle of an active function call stream. - tests/integration/sse_partial_json_test.go: new deterministic and randomized tests that simulate chunk-split UTF-8 and nested JSON objects across many micro-batches. - docs/compat/notes_sse_chunking.md: notes for integrators about rescue-marker semantics and upgraded adapter behavior. Implementation notes: - The sanitizer is intentionally conservative: by default it only defers emission for spans <4KB and recent activity; longer objects are passed through to avoid unbounded buffering. - UTF-8 sequences and escape sequences are validated with a fast-path routine; malformed runs trigger a rescue-marker plus the raw bytes (so higher-level telemetry can decide). - The functioncall sentinel ordering change is idempotent for well-formed streams; adapters that already respect function_call_start/end are unchanged. Rollout and migration: - Feature gated behind runtime flag "sse.stream_sanitizer.enabled" (default: off in canary). - Canary rollout to 5% of hosts for 48 hours; telemetry checks for increased latency, increased memory use, and rescued-frame rate. - If rescue rate >0.1% or p95 latency regression >10% we will roll back the gate. Testing and validation: - CI: added unit tests for boundary heuristics and integration test harness that replays recorded truncated-chunk incidents. - Staging: ran long-run fuzz and compatibility suite against openai-compat adapter; observed 0 parity regressions and eliminated a previously reproducible tool-call-argument-drop case. - Load test: negligible CPU increase on average, tail latencies increased by ~3ms in worst-case (within SLO). Review summary and discussion: - Jordan: "Core heuristic looks reasonable; ensure the window size is configurable per-customer since some private deployments may accept higher buffering." Ava: "Added config path and documented VPC override in installer notes." - Maya: "What about cascade effects with autoscaler when many hosts buffer?" Ava: "Sanitizer uses soft memory caps per-worker and will spill to disk after soft cap; we added a metric sse.sanitizer.spill_count and autoscaler alerts to the rollouts." - Ethan: "Adapter changes look safe but add a migration note for older clients." Ava: "Added compatibility shim to adapter.py that keeps old behavior if client declares legacy-mode." CI and merge: - CI initially caught a flaky test in the randomized harness; fixed by tightening the timing window and re-running. - All checks passed on the final run (unit, integration, lint, format, OSS compatibility). - Merge method: squash and merge. Related: ENG-4523 (streaming reliability), SUP-1290 (customer incident report). Release notes: "Fixes where SSE chunking could split JSON or function-call sentinels, causing truncated structured output for some OpenAI-compat consumers. Introduces a stream sanitizer and rescue-marker semantics; disabled by default during canary rollout." Risks and mitigations: "Increased memory under pathological inputs (mitigated via soft cap and spill-to-disk), and potential small p95 latency increase (observed <=3ms in load tests). Rollback plan included. Follow-ups: add server-side telemetry to track rescued-span types and a developer CLI to reassemble rescued frames for debugging.

title: Policy engine: add priority classes and per-route budgets (deterministic decision logging + validation)
preview: Summary: This PR extends the policy engine to support (a) explicit priority classes (enterprise/standard/best-effort/internal) and (b) per-route budgets that can be consumed deterministically during admission control / overload protection. It also adds deterministic decision logging (stable reason codes + structured fields) and a schema validation layer to keep policies auditable for enterprise escalations. Motivation: Hot-route capacity protection requires a single, explainable place to answer: "given tenant, route group, and current capacity signals, should we admit / throttle / shed / fallback". We need to do this with minimal per-request overhead and without introducing high-cardinality telemetry. This PR focuses on correctness + determinism; performance tuning (memoization/batched lookups) will follow. What’s in scope: 1) Policy schema additions: - priority_class: enum {INTERNAL, ENTERPRISE, STANDARD, BEST_EFFORT} (default STANDARD for backward compatibility) - route_group: canonical name for grouping endpoints (e.g., chat_completions, embeddings, rerank) - budgets: optional per route_group budgets keyed by priority class (requests/sec + burst + optional concurrency cap) - actions: allow | throttle | shed | fallback (fallback gated; final enforcement happens in gateway/routing) 2) Policy evaluation contract: - input includes: tenant_id, tier, route, route_group, region, and coarse capacity signals (queue_depth_bucket, gpu_saturation_bucket, scheduler_lag_bucket) - output includes: decision, decision_reason, retry_after_ms (optional), chosen_priority_class, matched_rule_id, and a stable decision_hash for auditability 3) Deterministic decision logging: - new "policy_decision" log event emitted by the policy engine with stable, low-cardinality fields - reason codes normalized to align with 429 semantics work in gateway (QUOTA_EXCEEDED vs OVERLOAD_SHED vs ADMISSION_THROTTLE vs FALLBACK_APPLIED) 4) Validation + guardrails: - schema validation on policy load (reject unknown enums/invalid windows) - explicit checks preventing per-tenant/per-route unconstrained overrides unless allowlisted (prevents accidental fleet-wide noisy-neighbor exceptions) - budget sanity checks (burst >= rate, non-negative, max caps) 5) Tests: - golden tests for deterministic output (given the same input policy + request context, decision_hash and reason fields must match) - validation tests covering invalid policies and non-backwards-compatible changes - basic evaluation tests covering priority ordering and budget exhaustion behavior Notes on backwards compatibility: - Existing policies without priority_class/route_group continue to evaluate as STANDARD with route_group derived from route mapping if available; if not available, route_group="unknown" and budgets are not applied. - Decision logging is added but does not change existing endpoints; log event is separate from request logs. Implementation details: - Added a policy schema version bump (v7 -> v8) with a translator that fills defaults on load. - Added a small canonical route->route_group mapping table co-located with schema; enforcement services should treat route_group as authoritative once provided in request context. - Budget accounting is modeled as an input signal ("budget_remaining_bucket") for now; actual counters are managed by gateway/routing. This PR sets the language/types and ensures evaluation is deterministic. Security/Access: - Deterministic decision logging includes tenant_id but is restricted to internal logs; no customer PII. - No new external endpoints. Follow-ups (tracked separately): - Performance: caching / batch lookups for high-QPS (ENG-4924/ENG-4925 dependent, plus upcoming perf PR). - Unify reason-code taxonomy and headers with gateway implementation once landed (paired with Amara/Cole). - Add admin-only debug endpoint to inspect decisions (INT-3302) once access controls are in place. References: - Linear: ENG-4923 - Docs/Design: Hot-route capacity protection design + ADR (see Confluence links in ENG-4923) - Related PRs: gateway 429 reason codes + routing admission control will consume decision_reason + priority_class.

final enforcement happens in gateway/routing) 2) Policy evaluation contract: - input includes: tenant_id, tier, route, route_group, region, and coarse capacity signals (queue_depth_bucket, gpu_saturation_bucket, scheduler_lag_bucket) - output includes: decision, decision_reason, retry_after_ms (optional), chosen_priority_class, matched_rule_id, and a stable decision_hash for auditability 3) Deterministic decision logging: - new "policy_decision" log event emitted by the policy engine with stable, low-cardinality fields - reason codes normalized to align with 429 semantics work in gateway (QUOTA_EXCEEDED vs OVERLOAD_SHED vs ADMISSION_THROTTLE vs FALLBACK_APPLIED) 4) Validation + guardrails: - schema validation on policy load (reject unknown enums/invalid windows) - explicit checks preventing per-tenant/per-route unconstrained overrides unless allowlisted (prevents accidental fleet-wide noisy-neighbor exceptions) - budget sanity checks (burst >= rate, non-negative, max caps) 5) Tests: - golden tests for deterministic output (given the same input policy + request context, decision_hash and reason fields must match) - validation tests covering invalid policies and non-backwards-compatible changes - basic evaluation tests covering priority ordering and budget exhaustion behavior Notes on backwards compatibility: - Existing policies without priority_class/route_group continue to evaluate as STANDARD with route_group derived from route mapping if available; if not available, route_group="unknown" and budgets are not applied. - Decision logging is added but does not change existing endpoints; log event is separate from request logs. Implementation details: - Added a policy schema version bump (v7 -> v8) with a translator that fills defaults on load. - Added a small canonical route->route_group mapping table co-located with schema; enforcement services should treat route_group as authoritative once provided in request context. - Budget accounting is modeled as an input signal ("budget_remaining_bucket") for now; actual counters are managed by gateway/routing. This PR sets the language/types and ensures evaluation is deterministic. Security/Access: - Deterministic decision logging includes tenant_id but is restricted to internal logs; no customer PII. - No new external endpoints. Follow-ups (tracked separately): - Performance: caching / batch lookups for high-QPS (ENG-4924/ENG-4925 dependent, plus upcoming perf PR). - Unify reason-code taxonomy and headers with gateway implementation once landed (paired with Amara/Cole). - Add admin-only debug endpoint to inspect decisions (INT-3302) once access controls are in place. References: - Linear: ENG-4923 - Docs/Design: Hot-route capacity protection design + ADR (see Confluence links in ENG-4923) - Related PRs: gateway 429 reason codes + routing admission control will consume decision_reason + priority_class.

title: Slab concatenation + FlashMixer kernel with async KV coalescer and continuous-batching horizon
preview: Context: Operator-level profiling in the last quarter showed sustained stalls on small-to-medium sequence lengths due to scatter/gather overheads and suboptimal KV warmup for bursty traffic. This PR introduces a new attention strategy (SlabConcatenate + FlashMixer microkernel) that reduces per-token overhead for heterogeneous sequence distributions and a lightweight async-KV coalescer that amortizes KV cache population across micro-batches. Motivation: 1) Reduce small-batch latency and increase throughput at mid-sequence lengths (256-2048) where customer workloads spend most of their tokens. 2) Retain deterministic rotary/rope semantics while enabling a fused concatenate path that avoids extra memcpy for tiled KV layouts. 3) Improve continuous batching horizon so runtime can opportunistically stretch batching without violating latency SLOs. What this change does: - Introduces FlashMixer microkernel: a variant of Flash-attention style tiling that mixes slab-concatenated Q/K/V regions to minimize memory fragmentation and enable a single fused rotary application pass. Optimized for 8-16 head widths on A100/RTX7x. - Adds SlabConcatenate path to the kernel selector with heuristics based on sequence length distribution and current batch skew metric. This path fuses concatenation, rotary application, and QK matmul into one kernel for contiguous slabs. - Implements Async KV Coalescer: a runtime stage that coalesces concurrent KV writes from overlapping micro-batches into a single GPU write and exposes a KV warmup metric. Coalescer has backpressure safety to avoid overpopulation for very long sequences. - Extends continuous batching horizon: runtime now computes an elastic horizon that trades a small p99 latency tail against higher throughput; this is governed by a new config: continuous_batching.max_horizon_ms and continuous_batching.tail_target_pct. - Preservation of model semantics: fused rotary/rope ops are designed to be numerically identical to the existing path (FP16 accumulation path validated). Testing & benchmarks: - Microbenchmarks (internal harness) show 9-14% throughput uplift and 6-10% median latency reduction at seq=512/1024 for T5-like models and ~12% on LLaMA-variant runs with head dim 64. - KV-heavy chat workload (continuous small messages) showed 18% cost improvement due to reduced redundant KV writes. - Extensive regression tests validate logits and sampling for baseline prompts and prompt sets in eval harness. Rollout plan: gated feature behind runtime flag slab_concat.enabled; rollout to canary clusters for 2 weeks; if no regressions, flip on in dedicated pools with auto-fallback to legacy path. Related: ENG-4821 (kv warmup telemetry), ENG-4899 (batching SLOs), SUP-3210 (customer-reported chat latency spike). Notes: this PR intentionally avoids global allocator changes and keeps the fused kernel opt-in. title: unified tool-caller API + delayed-commit retry semantics to stabilize streaming and multi-tool results
preview: Context and motivation: Customers frequently surface two pain points when composing LLM responses with external tools: 1) streaming instability when tool results and model tokens interleave mid-stream, and 2) duplicate side-effects from naive retry semantics for parallel tool calls. This PR introduces a unified 'Tool Caller' API and a delayed-commit retry strategy to address both issues in a developer-friendly, OpenAI-compatible surface. Design summary: The Tool Caller provides a single entrypoint for: a) orchestrating ordered or parallel tool invocations with deterministic merge semantics, b) registering streaming checkpoints so the model can safely stream partial token output without observing incomplete tool side-effects, and c) delayed-commit retries that only expose tool results to the model after idempotency checks pass. Streaming stability is handled via per-tool stream checkpoints plus a small bounded rebuffer window to preserve low-latency streaming while avoiding mid-stream observable state changes. The retry model uses idempotency tokens and a delayed commit phase: tool runs return provisional results that are not yet injected into the model stream; on success and idempotency confirmation we atomically commit the result into the model's context. Implementation highlights: server/api/tool_caller.py defines the ToolCaller class and public HTTP/SDK surface. server/streaming/stream_manager.py implements streaming checkpoints, rebuffer window, and backpressure integration. server/runtime/tool_dispatcher.cpp contains the low-level executor changes for parallel task orchestration and idempotency hooks. SDK shims (sdk/python/redwood/tools.py and sdk/typescript/src/tool-client.ts) expose the new call signature while preserving the old openai-compatible helpers via a compatibility layer. Observability: introduced tool_caller_latency and tool_commit_success metrics and added a small dashboard tile. Tests: new integration test (tests/integration/tool_composition_test.py) covers ordered, parallel, failure+retry, and streaming-snapshot scenarios. CI: added a targeted e2e workflow ci/workflows/tool-e2e.yml. Migration notes: The compatibility layer keeps old helper names; consumers that depended on streaming-mid-tool injections should migrate to the new two-phase commit injection model. See docs/migration/tool-caller-migration.md for a step-by-step guide and sample code. Rollout plan: feature-gate the new API for hosted and dedicated deployments for four weeks, shadow traffic for two days, then enable for 10% of main traffic for a week before full ramp. Monitoring will focus on commit latency, duplicate-effect rate, and streaming rebuffer tail latency. Backout plan: revert feature flag and disable delayed commits; hotfix branch created if we see increased error budgets. Benchmarks: internal perf runs show median end-to-end latency increase of ~6ms for single-tool calls due to idempotency checks, but multi-tool parallel calls saw a 22% reduction in observed duplicate side-effects and a 12% improvement in 99th percentile streaming stability in our multiplexing demo. Checklist: - [x] API spec and SDK shims implemented - [x] Observability added - [x] Integration tests added - [x] Migration guide written - [x] Benchmarks and rollout plan documented QA and verification steps: run example-apps/multiplexing-demo against local stack, validate no duplicate side-effects for simulated retries, run tool-composition integration test under stress, verify CI e2e pass. Security review: idempotency tokens are scoped per-customer and rotated via KMS; we added a short section in docs on token lifecycle. Known limitations: long-running tools that stream their own state must adopt the provisional-result pattern; we added examples in the migration doc. Relationship to other work: builds on ENG-2191 (tool orchestration primitives) and complements ENG-2248 (streaming stability improvements).

title: Add KMS throttle budget + request backpressure to prevent KMS saturation under spikes
preview:
Context: We’ve seen intermittent elevated 5xx correlated with upstream KMS throttling (SUP-19377) and a near-miss retry amplification event that required a hotfix (pr-18563). While the wrapper now has sane retry/jitter defaults and circuit breaking, we still had a failure mode where a large burst of decrypt/envelope ops (e.g. cache cold start + traffic spike) could saturate KMS and extend the incident blast radius.

This PR introduces an explicit "KMS throttle budget" and integrates KMS calls with the existing request admission/backpressure path so we shed/queue work earlier rather than stampeding KMS.

What’s included (high level): (1) Per-process token-bucket budget for KMS operations: A lightweight budget that is decremented on KMS requests and refilled at a configured rate. When depleted, KMS calls are delayed (bounded) or rejected with a typed error depending on caller policy. This keeps concurrency and request rate within a predictable envelope even if upstream is degraded. (2) Adaptive refill + penalty on throttling: When KMS returns throttling (e.g., AWS ThrottlingException / 429 equivalents), we apply an immediate penalty to the budget refill rate for a short window. This quickly reduces pressure and avoids repeated throttles. (3) Backpressure integration: The KMS client wrapper now plugs into `admission.Controller` so callers can either: (a) block up to `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` and then fail fast, or (b) fail immediately (for latency-sensitive paths) with a clear error category (`KmsBudgetExhausted`). (4) Metrics + tracing: Exposes budget state and decisions, and tags spans with the backpressure outcome.

Config knobs (all have safe defaults; prod guardrails still enforced by pr-18502): - `kms.budget.refill_rps` (default: 200 ops/sec) - `kms.budget.burst` (default: 400 ops) - `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` (default: 50ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_window_ms` (default: 30000ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_factor` (default: 0.5)

Behavioral notes: - This budget is per-process (not global) by design. It limits local amplification and works well with our horizontal scaling/admission control. If we need global limits later, we can layer it via control plane config + per-pool budgets. - We do NOT silently drop decrypts. If we can’t meet the wait bound, we return a typed error and let the caller’s policy decide whether to fail the request, fall back, or rely on cached material (where allowed). - When combined with the circuit breaker, the system now transitions more cleanly: budget starts slowing requests first; if KMS remains degraded, the breaker opens and callers follow degrade-mode policy.

Implementation details: - `kmsbudget.Bucket`: token-bucket with penalty hooks; uses monotonic clock; jittered sleeps to avoid herd effects at refill boundaries. - `kmsclient.Client`: wraps `Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey` calls with `Acquire(ctx)`; all outcomes emit structured logs and metrics. - `admission.Controller`: added a dependency-specific limiter hook (KMS only for now) so we can reuse the backpressure path without leaking KMS specifics into product code.

Metrics added: - `redwood_kms_budget_tokens{env,region,service}` (gauge) - `redwood_kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome="immediate|waited|timeout|rejected"}` (counter) - `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms` (histogram) - `redwood_kms_throttle_penalty_active` (gauge) - Existing `redwood_kms_errors_total{category=...}` is now incremented for `BudgetExhausted` and `BackpressureTimeout` (maps to the canonical taxonomy).

Example log fields (new/standardized): `kms_op`, `kms_budget_outcome`, `kms_budget_wait_ms`, `kms_budget_tokens_after`, `kms_error_category`, `trace_id`.

Testing: - Unit tests for token bucket correctness and penalty behavior (including time skews). - Integration harness updated to simulate 429/throttling and verify that request rate to the KMS stub decreases within 2s and that tail latency stays bounded (ties into ENG-4825). - Added a regression test ensuring we don’t exceed retry+budget wait combined latency cap.

Rollout plan: - Behind `kms.backpressure.enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until we flip per environment). - Canary in us-east Dedicated + Hosted data-plane: monitor `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms p95`, KMS 429 rate, and request 5xx. - Flip prod flag gradually per region; rollback is a single flag change.

Checklist: - [x] SRE reviewed paging/threshold implications (no new pages by default; dashboards use derived panels) - [x] Security reviewed failure semantics (no weakening of envelope decrypt correctness) - [x] Added metrics + trace tags per observability conventions - [x] Added tests for throttle + backpressure scenarios

Follow-ups: - Consider exposing recommended budget defaults in the Private operator guide once we validate prod behavior across 2+ regions. - Evaluate whether control plane should push pool-specific budgets for Dedicated customers with known KMS quotas.

Related: ENG-4821, ENG-4825, ENG-4828; incident context SUP-19377; complements pr-18563 (retry storm hotfix), pr-18449 (circuit breaker), pr-18502 (config guardrails).

title: adaptive qk-kv fusion with prefetching and rotary interleave for long-context throughput
preview: Motivation: Long-context workloads (>=16k tokens) dominate several customer workloads. Existing FlashAttention and separate QK/KV kernels yield suboptimal memory traffic when serving long sequences with continuous batching and active KV caching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel strategy that fuses QK compute and KV accumulation paths where beneficial, plus a lightweight KV prefetcher and a rotary-interleave layout to reduce memory bandwidth and kernel launch overhead. Design: 1) New fused kernels (CUDA) that compute QK scores and simultaneously write/aggregate KV fragments for future decode steps. These kernels are selected when sequence-length, batch composition, and kv cache hit heuristics indicate net win. 2) Adaptive kernel dispatcher: runtime heuristics (histogram of seq lengths, batch fill factor, GPU occupancy) choose between FlashAttention variants, fused QK-KV path, or the legacy split kernels. 3) KV prefetcher: background thread schedules small prefetches of cache pages for the most likely next-tokens based on route-level bandit policy, reducing tail latency on cache-miss heavy workloads. 4) Rotary interleave: reorganizes rotary embeddings in-memory to make fused kernels use contiguous loads for q/k/v, reducing strided accesses on long context. Implementation notes: - CUDA kernels implemented in runtime/attention with careful register/block tuning for Ampere/Blackwell GPUs. - Dispatcher lives in runtime/attention/kernel_dispatcher.cc and exposes a policy hook for testing via env vars. - Continuous batcher updated to expose batch shape histograms and to annotate requests with expected next-token probabilities (used by prefetcher). - Small pinned-memory prefetch buffer added to runtime/kv_cache, with heuristics to avoid polluting hot-cache entries. Tests & validation: microbenchmarks (tests/benchmarks/attn_long_context.py) show median throughput gain of 18% on 32k-context benchmarks vs previous flash-attn-only baseline, p50 decode latency reduced 12% for batched streaming, and token-cost reduction of ~9% under production-like mixes due to fewer kernel launches and lower GPU memory traffic. We ran full GPU integration tests, perf canary, and private deployment smoke tests. Rollout plan: feature gated behind env REDWOOD_FEATURE_ADAPTIVE_FUSE and opt-in in Dedicated as default off for first 2 weeks. Safety: we added deterministic fallbacks and correctness unit tests comparing fused outputs to reference split-kernel outputs for a variety of dtype/seq-length/model-size combinations. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. See release_notes for user-visible improvements.

title: decouple-serializer-from-affinity-and-introduce-cooperative-serialize-queues
preview: Motivation: our telemetry and recent incident traces show a non-trivial CPU-side bottleneck in the request hotpath caused by blocking serialization work that holds thread-affinity (OS-level pinning) and prevents efficient cooperative scheduling between IO, serializer, and runtime workers. This PR separates the concerns: (1) introduce a lightweight cooperative serialization queue that runs on worker threads without requiring permanent thread pinning, (2) decouple the serializer state machine from thread-affinity so short critical sections can opportunistically run on any available worker, and (3) add backpressure signals to the queue which feed into the scheduler to dynamically throttle batch assembly when CPU saturation is detected. Design summary: add runtime/serialize_queue.{h,cpp} implementing a fixed-size ring queue with lock-free handover for small payloads and a CAS-protected overflow path for large messages. Add a SerializerActor that owns transient per-request buffers but is not bound to a specific CPU core; a short move-to-stack and memcpy micro-optimization keeps copies minimal. Thread pool changes: make ThreadPool::pinning optional at task-granularity, introduce ThreadPool::submit_cooperative(task) which participates in the cooperative scheduling loop. KV cache interaction: when a request hits KV cache for a prefix, the cache lookup now signals the serialize queue to short-circuit full marshalling when cached prefix covers >70% of tokens. Rollout strategy: feature-flag guarded, defaults off for Dedicated regions until we run a gated canary. Benchmarks: integration perf runs on our internal perf-lab show median tail (P95) endpoint latency improvement of 9.2% on mixed small/medium requests, median CPU sys-time for request workers fell from 42% to 29% under the same load profile (16 concurrent clients), and end-to-end tokens/sec improved by 12% for continuous-batching workloads due to reduced serialization stalls. Safety and testing: unit tests for the queue and overflow path, stress tests with randomized task yields, and end-to-end perf harness runs included. Compatibility: no API change to external clients. Rollback plan: flip feature flag and revert the small patch in main that wires up the queue. Notes: this is intentionally conservative on the overflow path to avoid introducing GC/alloc churn in the hotpath.

title: Introduce lightweight tracepoint sampler and batch-pressure microbench for scheduler visibility
preview:
Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to correlate scheduler decisions, KV-cache hot prefixes, and continuous-batching pressure in production-like workloads. Existing NVTX and perfetto exports are useful but either too heavy or too coarse for iterative scheduler tuning. This PR introduces a lightweight tracepoint sampler plus a compact microbench harness that reproduces batch-pressure scenarios seen in customer workloads.

What this PR does: 1) Introduces a TracepointSampler module (C++): periodic, low-payload tracepoints emitted around scheduler windowing and key KV-cache touchpoints. Sampling is rate-limited and context-tagged so it is safe to enable in Dedicated/Private pockets for short experiments. 2) Adds a BatchPressureMicrobench: a small harness that simulates mixed arrival patterns (burst+poisson), tracks batch fill/evict cycles, and reports per-step metrics (queue depth, avg-batch-size, scheduler-latency). 3) Adds a lightweight exporter template that converts sampler output to a compact CSV/JSON line format and a dashboard YAML for the perf observability stack. 4) Provides unit and integration tests for correctness and a CI bench job to run the microbench in controlled runners.

Design notes: - TracepointSampler emits an inline 64-bit tag plus epoch counter; it avoids heavy NVTX payloads to keep overhead < 0.2% in our microbench. - The sampler can be configured by percent-sampling or deterministic-epoch windows; configuration lives under runtime.conf under [tracepoint_sampler]. - The bench harness focuses on reproducing scheduler backpressure (when batches are delayed waiting for KV-cache or kernel queues). It is intentionally small so it can be run in CI and locally.

Benchmarks: Local runs on an r5-like simulated env show: - 0.12% median runtime overhead with sampler enabled at 1% sampling. - Microbench reproduces a clear inflection when batch-timeouts exceed 6ms and shows scheduler queue growth matching production traces. Included CSV outputs for two representative runs in bench/outputs/*.csv.

Testing & CI: - Added unit tests for sampler tagging and epoch wrap-around. - Added a bench CI job (ci/bench/bench-runner.yml) that executes microbench in a small container; results are archived as artifacts. CI initially failed due to a missing clock dependency in the test harness; fixed in second commit.

Migration / Rollout: This is a safe, opt-in instrumentation path. Default sampling is disabled in production configs. We recommend: 1) enable 0.1% sampling on a canary Dedicated node for 24 hours, 2) collect artifacts and load into the dashboard template, 3) iterate sampling/window params and run microbench locally to validate hypotheses.

Follow-ups: - Wire sampler output to the existing perf-trace ingestion pipeline (ENG-1185). - Add a lightweight JS/TS helper to download and visualise CSVs in the console (ENG-1190).

Related: builds on prior instrumentation work but intentionally avoids heavy NVTX payloads and Perfetto dependency so we can run in constrained CI nodes. See related PRs: ENG-93547-style batching probes and ENG-93421 (kv-cache sim) for context.

title: Adaptive kernel routing and KV-scheduler tweaks for Mistral/Qwen (GQA/MQA tuning)
preview: Motivation: production workloads showed inconsistent latency/throughput tradeoffs across model families (Mistral, Qwen, Llama variants) when using a single kernel selection and KV cache routing policy. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel routing layer and a lightweight KV-scheduler that chooses sharding and prefetch priorities per model and request workload, plus targeted GQA/MQA tuning knobs. The goals are: reduce p50/p95 latency variance, increase sustained throughput for mixed-sequence traffic, and avoid tail regressions when switching model variants. Summary of changes (single-line condensed): implement model profiles for kernel selection and quantization-aware algorithms; add KV-scheduler with per-route priority and prefetch window; continuous-batching changes to respect model affinity and early-flush heuristics; small Llama-specific attention correction for 8-bit MQA flows; Qwen memory layout workaround to avoid kernel thrash on long-context streams; add microbench harness + regression tests + docs updates. Checklist: 1) Perf harness and baseline numbers included 2) Unit tests for scheduler logic 3) Integration canary on perf-canary passed 4) Added release notes and console docs 5) Backwards-compatible config defaults. Commits summary: 1) "model-profiles: add adaptive kernel routing and profile schema" 2) "runtime: KV-scheduler implementation and tests" 3) "batcher: affinity and early-flush integration" 4) "mistral: 8-bit attention edge-case fix" 5) "qwen: continuous-kernel fallback for long context" 6) "docs: runtime tuning guide and release notes" Changed areas (high-level file list): runtime/kernel_router.cpp; runtime/kernel_selector.h; runtime/kv_scheduler.{cc,h}; runtime/batching/continuous_batcher.cpp; models/mistral/attention_fixes.cpp; models/qwen/kernel_fallback.cpp; perf/bench/kv_sched_harness.py; docs/runtime/optimizations.md; tests/runtime/test_kv_scheduler.py. Small illustrative snippets (kept minimal): added selection score calculation in kernel_router.cpp to prefer quantization-aware kernels for MQA flows; KV-scheduler uses LRU-backed prefetch window with a per-model weight. Tests and CI: initial CI run hit an intermittent GPU smoke failure in integration/gpu-smoke (nvidia-alloc-timeout), retried and passed; full perf-canary run showed 6-12% median latency reduction for Mistral-7B workloads (p50 down 8% median), and 18-24% increase in sustained tokens/sec for mixed short+long sequences on Qwen-14B when MQA profile enabled. Llama 2-13B 8-bit flows showed a 4% p95 improvement after the attention edge-case fix. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout for 2 weeks on dedicated cluster subset, followed by staged enablement per customer for private deployments; follow-up ENG task to instrument model-profile telemetry and auto-tune heuristics. Backwards compatibility: default kernel_router policy remains legacy until feature flag is enabled; config knobs documented. Release notes: performance: adaptive kernel routing and KV-scheduler reduce latency variance and improve throughput across Mistral/Qwen/LLM families; fixes: Mistral 8-bit attention correctness; notes: may improve or change token cost estimates due to different kernel selection. Related tickets: ENG-7684 (runtime perf initiative), ENG-7721 (KV scheduling design).

title: scheduler: jittered slot reassignment and KV-warmup throttling
preview: Context: In high-concurrency mixed workloads we observed correlated slot reclamations and KV cache warmups causing transient starvation and p95 latency spikes. This PR implements two complementary runtime controls: (1) jittered slot reassignment to spread slot release/reassignment events across short randomized windows (configurable), and (2) a KV-warmup throttler that staggers cold-KV prefetches and enforces a soft admission budget per-slot to avoid simultaneous heavy prefetches. Changes: Implements a new scheduler helper (jitter_reassign) which inserts small randomized offsets before slot handoff and coordinates with the slot allocator to avoid churn; implements kv_warmup_throttle which tracks per-model warmup tokens and decays budgets based on successful cache hits; exposes scheduler.tuning.jitter_ms and kv.warmup.budget_rate config knobs; updates continuous_batcher admission path to consult warmup budget before immediate prefetch; adds lightweight metrics (jitter.delay_ms distribution, kv.warmup.throttle_rejections) and dashboard panels. Commits: "add jittered slot reassignment helper"; "implement kv warmup token bucket and decay"; "plumb knobs through config and expose metrics"; "perf tests and bench harness for mixed workloads". Changed files (high-level): runtime/scheduler/jitter_reassign.go; runtime/scheduler/slot_allocator.go (small API); runtime/kv/warmup_throttle.go; runtime/batcher/continuous_batcher.go (admission path); metrics/registry.go (new metrics); docs/benchmarks/jitter_warmup_readme.md; tests/perf/test_jittered_reassign.py; tests/unit/test_kv_warmup_throttle.go; ci/workflows/perf.yml. Motivation: Previous approaches (hard timeouts and global cooldowns) either caused head-of-line blocking or were too coarse-grained for multi-tenant bursts. The jittered reassignment spreads out reassignments to reduce synchronized pressure on the slot allocator and downstream kernel selection; warmup throttling prevents cache stampedes and reduces wasted kernel invocations. Performance: On internal perf harness using a 64-GPU cluster and mixed 75% short, 25% long requests we saw median latency change ~+2ms, p95 reduced ~18%, p99 reduced ~10%, and KV cache miss storms reduced by ~42% in burst scenarios. CPU/GPU utilization improved: fewer small kernel startups and more sustained larger kernels resulting in 6% lower cost/throughput token. Testing: unit tests for token decay and edge cases; perf tests covering staggered bursts, tenant fairness, and long-tail KV loads; load test in staging with warmup budget default of 10 tokens/sec. CI: initial perf run showed a flaky bench; reran perf pipeline with increased harness stabilization and all checks passed. Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind runtime.scheduler.jittered_reassign (default enabled for dedicated clusters, opt-in for hosted at 10% rollout), staged ramp: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with automated rollback on regression signals (p95 increase >5% or error rates >0.1%). Backout: config toggle and immediate revert PR prepared if needed. Related: references ENG-4219 for prioritized scheduling work, ENG-4291 for KV cache prefetch follow-up. Checklist: design doc attached in Linear; unit tests added; perf benchmark included; docs updated.

title: Reduce CPU syscalls and thread handoff on request path
preview: Motivation: CPU-side bottlenecks on the request hot path were dominated by frequent syscalls and expensive thread handoffs when requests crossed the network/IO boundary. This PR reduces syscall frequency and simplifies handoff between acceptor/dispatcher threads and worker pools to lower tail latency and overall CPU usage. Summary of approach: 1) Replace a large fraction of blocking read()/write()/select() syscalls on the hot path with an edge-triggered epoll-based poll loop + per-core submission queues to amortize kernel transitions; 2) Introduce a light-weight per-core MPMC ring buffer (lock-free where supported) used to hand off ready requests to CPU-local worker shards instead of parking/unparking threads; 3) Move request serialization (marshalling) off the acceptor path onto a short-lived serialization worker that batches small requests together (continuous batching window = 1.5ms configurable) to reduce per-request syscall and kernel scheduling pressure; 4) Make KV cache lookups prefetch-friendly and avoid cacheline thrash by introducing per-shard micro-ttl caches and partitioned LRU lists; 5) Reduce futex/wake cycles by aggregating wake events using a shared event-coalescer and using eventfd on Linux where available. Results: On our internal bench (conv1k latency-heavy mix) we see a 28-35% reduction in 99th percentile CPU time spent in syscall/wakeup handling and a 12-18% improvement in overall token throughput on c5d-large simulation (worker-limited). Tail latency (p99.9) decreased by ~9% for small requests (<128 tokens). Backwards compatibility and safety: All changes are behind a feature flag (runtime.enable_syscall_reduction) and fall back to the original path when disabled. New primitives are exercised via unit + integration tests, and we added a runtime smoke test that toggles the feature flag under load. Details of changes: commits, files touched, and notes below.

title: Introduce jurisdiction-aware assertion proxy, KMS consent router, and audit enrichment shim
preview: Motivation: customers with mixed-residency fleets asked for a deterministic place to evaluate operator/service jurisdiction assertions so KMS usage and audit provenance can be routed safely without sprinkling logic across services. This PR introduces an assertion proxy (service: residency-assertor) that normalizes incoming claims, evaluates configured jurisdiction policies, and emits a compact proof token used by downstream KMS routing and audit enrichment. It also adds a KMS Consent Router to pick an appropriate CMK/region and a lightweight audit-enrichment shim that attaches pseudonymous residency and proof metadata to audit events. Implementation summary: 1) residency-assertor service (new binary) with a small policy DSL converted into OPA-compatible JSON; 2) kms-consent-router middleware integrated into the existing kms-proxy client path with a decision cache and TTL; 3) audit-enricher sidecar that subscribes to commit log and augments events with residency_proof and audit_cohort tags; 4) feature flags and rollout controls (config path: /deploy/feature-flags/jurisdiction_assertion.json) and a canary rollout plan. Security and compliance notes: decisions compute a minimal proof (HMAC-signed) containing issuer, asserted_region, policy_id and exp; the HMAC key is stored in KMS and rotated via existing rotation pipeline; audit enrichment strips any direct tenant-identifiers and emits cohort hashes to maintain investigatability without exposing PII. Backwards compatibility: all services fall back to existing routing when the feature flag is off or residency-assertor returns no-decision. Performance: decision cache and async enrichment keep tail latency impact <5ms p95 on request path in our local benchmarks. Checklist: - [x] Policy DSL parser and unit tests - [x] kms-consent-router integrated and e2e tests - [x] audit-enricher implemented with audit schema migration + migration test harness - [x] Feature flag gating and canary rollout manifest - [x] Documentation: runbook + threat model note and operator guide - [x] Added telemetry: rbac_decisionlatencies, kms_routing_hits, audit_enrich_failures Rollout plan: staged canary to 5% tenants in region-us then 20% then global over two weeks; automated rollback triggers on >1% enrichment failures or >10% request-latency degradation. Related work: builds on ENG-991 (decision-cache) and SEC-88 (audit-envelope revamp). Links: linear/ENG-2345, internal-docs/ops/jurisdiction-rules. Testing: unit coverage 94% for new modules; integration pipeline added: unit -> lint -> e2e/kms-stub -> canary-simulation; CI transient: one flaky integration rerun but green on retry. Merge strategy and outcome: squash merge; post-merge migration job scheduled to backfill audit cohorts for 7 days. Notes for reviewers: pay attention to policy DSL escaping rules and HMAC key rotation path; reviewers asked to validate migration safety and to run the canary simulation tool locally.

title: token-span-attribution-and-adaptive-alert-smoothing
preview:
Motivation:
We currently surface token-level cost and latency aggregations but lack a clear way to attribute SLO impact to specific token spans (e.g. model prompt prefix vs generated tail) and we get frequent alert flapping on percentile SLOs when low-volume, high-cost tails show up. This PR introduces two complementary capabilities: 1) span-level token attribution traces and rollups keyed by token position buckets (prefix/mid/suffix) and cache-affinity (kv-hit vs kv-miss); and 2) an adaptive alert-smoothing layer that reduces noise for route-level token-cost SLOs using a decayed-reservoir quantile estimator that weights tokens by cost and recency.

What this changes (high level):
- Runtime telemetry: emit token-level spans with attributes: token_index, span_bucket (prefix|mid|suffix), kv_affinity (hit|miss), token_cost_units. These are synthesized as aggregated metrics and lightweight trace annotations to avoid high-cardinality explosion.
- Metrics rollups: new Prometheus/OpenMetrics series and Cortex rollup pipeline config to produce: rwd_token_span_cost_sum, rwd_token_span_cost_count, rwd_token_span_latency_p75/p95/p99 per route and per span_bucket.
- Sampling strategy: a token-aware reservoir sampler in the tracing pipeline that biases retention probability proportionally to token_cost_units and recent latency anomalies to preserve informative tails.
- Alerting: SLO rules updated to reference the smoothed quantiles produced by the adaptive estimator; alerting notifications include the top contributing span_bucket breakdown and a compact token-cost histogram snapshot.
- Console: observability UI adds a per-route Token Span panel with heatmap, selectable span_bucket filters, and a toggle to show raw vs smoothed SLO curves for a chosen time window.
- Docs/Quickstart: example notebook showing how to interpret span attribution and how to tune smoothing window for a given workload.

Implementation notes:
- The runtime emits a compact token-span protobuf (rwd.trace.TokenSpan) and a new lightweight counter for kv-affinity to keep cardinality low; the code path in serving/runtime/telemetry is hardened to avoid sync disk writes.
- Reservoir estimator implemented as a fixed-memory sketch using a weighted priority queue and exponential decay; state is materialized at the rollup tier and also emitted as a derivative metric so alert rules can reference it directly without additional query-time compute overhead.
- Backwards compatibility: when normalized flags are off the system falls back to previous token aggregation metrics (no name changes to legacy meters).

Checklist:
- [x] Instrument runtime token-span emission (serving/runtime/telemetry)
- [x] Rollup pipeline config and transformation (observability/rollups)
- [x] Add Prometheus export and Cortex rules (observability/metrics)
- [x] Tracing sampler implementation and unit tests (observability/tracing)
- [x] Console UI panels and integration tests (console/frontend)
- [x] Docs and quickstart (docs/observability)
- [x] Load test (benchmark-lab token-heavy scenario)

Commits summary:
- feat(telemetry): token-span proto + runtime emitter
- feat(rollups): transform and rollup keys for span_bucket and kv_affinity
- feat(tracing): weighted reservoir token sampler with decay
- feat(metrics): exporter and prometheus rules expose span-level aggregates
- feat(console): token-span heatmap and smoothed SLO toggle
- docs: observability token-span guide and tuning examples

Files changed (high level):
- redwood/serving/runtime/telemetry/token_span.proto
- redwood/serving/runtime/telemetry/emitter.go
- redwood/observability/rollups/span_rollup_transform.yaml
- redwood/observability/tracing/reservoir_sampler.go
- redwood/observability/metrics/prom_rules/token_span_rules.yaml
- redwood/console/frontend/src/components/TokenSpanPanel.tsx
- redwood/docs/observability/token-span.md
- benchmark-lab/scenarios/token-heavy/20k_requests.yaml
- redwood/quickstarts/observability/token-span-quickstart.ipynb

Testing and CI:
- Unit tests added for reservoir sampler edge cases (zero-weight, overflow) and rollup transformation invariants.
- End-to-end test in benchmark-lab simulating 10k requests with varying prompt lengths to validate sampler retention and alert smoothing behavior.
- CI: all automated checks passed on first run; load test executed in a post-merge pipeline.

Review conversation (high level):
- Diego Alvarez: "This addresses noise we've seen on /chat during the November spike. Two asks: add a small p50 metric for each span_bucket and ensure the sampler is configurable per-tenant."
- Author reply: "Added p50 rollup and moved sampler config to tenant-visible config with safe defaults. See emitter.go and config docs."
- Priya Natarajan: "Concern about metric cardinality -- how are we preventing explosion for many routes?"
- Author reply: "We only emit span_bucket and kv_affinity dimensions at the rollup layer and sample traces at a rate bounded by the reservoir size. The raw token spans are not indexed in the long-term TSDB."
- Liam O'Sullivan: "Minor: please include a console screenshot or gif in the docs for reviewers."
- Author reply: "Added a small animated gif and an annotated screenshot to docs/observability/token-span.md."

Merge notes:
- PR merged via squash after approvals from all reviewers. Post-merge: rolled out metric transforms to staging then production during a low-traffic window; no alerts fired during initial ramp. Load tests showed a 3.1% CPU increase on the telemetry emitter and a 0.6% increase in Cortex ingestion cost; both within acceptable bounds per PM-421.

Release notes:
- "Observability: Added token-span attribution metrics and adaptive alert-smoothing to reduce SLO noise and surface per-route span-level cost breakdowns. Console UI includes a Token Span heatmap and smoothed SLO visualization. Tunable sampler and tenant-level knobs included."

Rollback plan:
- Disable sampler flag (runtime config) and revert rollup transform; legacy metrics remain available.

Followups (open):
- ENG-8010: Add tenant-level retention budgeting for sampled token spans (scheduled Q1 2026)
- ENG-8041: Expose a per-route cost forecast using span attribution in Optimize recommendations

Notes for oncall:
- If SLO alerts go into a flapping pattern after deployment, toggle the alert-smoothing feature off via observability/feature_flags and open ENG-8010.

title: Introduce policy simulator with KMS proofing and audit-diff tooling
preview: Title: Introduce policy simulator with KMS proofing and audit-diff tooling. Motivation: Customers and internal auditors frequently ask for a way to validate RBAC v2 policy changes before rollout and to produce cryptographic proof that a proposed change does not widen region/KMS residency exposure. This PR adds a developer-facing simulator (rbac-sim) that evaluates a set of example requests against a candidate policy delta, emits a human- and machine-readable audit-diff, and (optionally) attaches a short-lived KMS-signed proof bundle that attests to the simulator inputs and outputs for downstream compliance workflows. This is intended to reduce post-deploy surprises, speed review cycles for Dedicated/Private deployments, and plug into our audit retention processes. Summary of changes (high level commits): 1) feat(simulator): introduce rbac-sim CLI and library with JSON policy delta apply and evaluation harness — high-level API, test suite and example fixtures (commit 1/6). 2) feat(audit-diff): add audit-diff generator that produces compact ndjson with decision-level deltas and counts for each service/operation combination (commit 2/6). 3) feat(kms-proof): add optional KMS proof signer that wraps the simulator run in a signed envelope (supporting both hosted KMS and customer CMKs via delegation tokens) (commit 3/6). 4) chore(ci): add unit and integration tests to workflow and container build step (commit 4/6). 5) docs: add quickstart and compliance playbook describing how to run sims against production policy snapshots and how to consume proof bundles (commit 5/6). 6) perf: small perf improvement to policy compile cache to keep simulator runs under 300ms for typical policy sets (commit 6/6). Changed areas/files (high level): - services/rbac/simulator/{main.go,simulator.go,policy_parser.go,fixtures/} - tools/rbac-sim/dockerfile and Makefile - libs/kms/proof_signer.go with integrations for hosted KMS and delegated CMK tokens - infra/ci/rbac-sim.yml (unit + policy integration tests) - docs/compliance/rbac-simulator.md and examples/compliance/playbook.md - testdata/policy-deltas/* (example deltas used in CI) Review notes and design decisions: - Simulator semantics intentionally mirror production PDP evaluation order; we reuse policy compile and the PDP cache. Edge cases for resource wildcards were clarified and documented. - KMS proofing is opt-in; the envelope contains a compact SHA256 of the input fixtures and the ndjson audit-diff, a timestamp, and a signer id. Proofs use existing hosted KMS signing key by default; for Dedicated/Private customers we accept a delegated CMK token and include the key fingerprint in the envelope metadata. - We modeled the audit-diff format around existing ndjson conventions used by the observability team so the output can be ingested into our log-retention and compliance pipelines without extra transformation. - Security review: ephemeral tokens for delegated CMK usage are validated against operator-scoped grants; we added additional guardrails to prevent simulator-initiated KMS requests from being used as an escalation vector. Testing and CI: - Unit tests added for policy parser edge cases, comparator and ndjson generation. - Integration test runs in CI spin up a minimal policy store and runs three representative deltas; artifacts are validated (ndjson schema + optional proof envelope signature verification). - CI checks passed on final run: build (pass), unit-tests (pass), integration-tests (pass), lint (pass). Review conversation (condensed into single timeline): - Ethan Chu (reviewer): "Nice to see a simulator — two asks: 1) make sure the simulator cannot call production KMS by default; 2) add a gated flag for proofing so that automated CI jobs don't accidentally sign artifacts with hosted keys." - Author (Priya): "Both addressed — default is dry-run-only and proofing requires explicit --enable-proof + env var pointing to KMS key alias. Added tests to assert the default behavior." - Marisol Vega (reviewer): "We should include a short retention policy recommendation for proof bundles — added a doc comment." - Samir Patel (security reviewer): "Verified token validation logic and put a small nit on log noise; asked for an additional audit check that the simulator writes a non-replayable nonce into the envelope." - Priya: "Added nonce and updated docs; also added an integration test that verifies nondeterminism across runs with the same inputs when nonce is present." - Merge readiness: reviewers approved after two rounds of fixes. CI green on approval. Observability and ops notes: - Simulator runs emit metrics under rbac.simulator.run.{success,failure,duration_ms} and proofing emits rbac.simulator.proof.sign.{success,failure}. - Console: added a minimal dashboard tile for recent simulator runs (docs include Grafana snippet). - Rollout plan: feature behind a feature flag rbac.simulator.enabled; default disabled in Hosted. Admins can enable per-tenant for Dedicated and Private installs. Compliance playbook: - How to run: ./tools/rbac-sim --policy-snapshot /path/to/snapshot --delta /path/to/delta.json --out audit-diff.ndjson --enable-proof --kms-key=alias/custom-cmk - How to review: the audit-diff groups changed allow/deny decisions by service and includes sample example requests that would change decision. Proof bundle contains base64(signed_envelope)+ndjson and a verification helper script for auditors. Migration and operational impact: - No runtime changes to PDP; simulator is a standalone tool and library. - Policy changes should still go through standard Linear flow; engineers are encouraged to attach audit-diff + proof to policy change PRs. - For Dedicated/Private customers, operator docs include steps to provide CMK delegation tokens and to configure short-term key usage. Security mitigations implemented: - Simulator KMS operations are rate-limited and annotated; we added a sandbox KMS path for Hosted to avoid accidental use of production keys in automated workflows. - Proof envelopes include signer id, key fingerprint, timestamp, nonce and a tight TTL (default 24h). - Added RBAC rule to prevent non-admin roles from creating proof-enabled simulator runs through the API wrapper. Known limitations and future work: - Current simulator validates policy deltas only; future work will support side-by-side multi-tenant scenarios where composite policy resolution is necessary. - We do not yet automatically attach simulator artifacts to Linear tickets; that will be a small follow-up (ENG-4369). Merge outcome: merged via squash/merge after approvals; release note drafted and added to changelog. Additional metadata: example command lines, proof envelope format and verification helper included in docs. Contact: priya@redwood.example for follow-ups.
