title: heterogeneous-attention-fusion-with-staged-scheduler-and-continuous-batching-support
preview: Motivation: reduce p99 latency regression for mixed-sequence workloads and improve throughput for production chat and retrieval runtimes by selecting optimal attention kernels at runtime and enabling continuous batching without sacrificing KV cache hit stability. Changes: Introduces a heterogeneous attention selection layer (HeteroAttn) that picks between a low-latency microkernel and a high-throughput Flash-like kernel based on sequence length heuristics and current queueing pressure; adds a staged scheduler that enforces a short-term reservation window for latency-sensitive requests and a batch-aggregation window for throughput requests; implements a cooperative KV cache eviction policy (staged LRU) that works with continuous batching to avoid repeated cache thrash during rollups; adds fused rotary/rope offset precomputation paths that avoid memcpy for common rotary patterns; integrates fp16 accumulation path with optional fp32 fallback for edge-case stability; exposes runtime knobs: ATTEN_SELECT_WINDOW_MS, KV_STAGED_WINDOW, CBATCH_MAX_WAIT_MS for tuned rollouts. Testing and validation: microbenchmarked on c6i-gpu clusters and internal perf-lab; added synthetic mixed-sequence workload harness and end-to-end chat replay traces. Results: median latency improved 6-9% on mid-sized seqs, p99 reduced up to 22% for mixed workloads, throughput increased 12% on long-sequence tails while KV miss rate decreased 18% under continuous batching. Rollout plan: staged canary on dedicated pool alpha -> 50% traffic after 72h, automatic rollback on p99 regressions >10% or error rate increase >0.5%. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. Known limitations: rare fp16 accumulation differences at extreme sequence lengths (documented and guarded by runtime flag), scheduler parameters tuned for our standard clusters; customers on small on-prem may require adjustments. Checklist: - [x] Benchmarks added to perf-lab harness - [x] Unit + integration tests for scheduler and KV policy - [x] Feature-flagged rollout - [x] Docs entry in runtime tuning guide - [x] Telemetry events for selection decisions

title: introduce-auth-tactician-ttl-prefetch-and-pullstream-refactor
preview: Context: Cross-SDK follow-up to earlier session/lease work — introduces an "auth tactician" component that centralizes token TTL awareness and a small prefetch policy to avoid tail latency for streamed pull/push APIs. Motivation: customers saw intermittent ~200-400ms spikes when long-running streams triggered last-second token refreshes; retry/backoff logic masked this but increased request churn and observable noise. Goal: reduce tail latency by proactively warming credentials within a TTL window, provide a small, well-documented opt-in prefetch policy across Python/TS/Go SDKs, and clean up PullStream ergonomics so the language SDKs expose a common surface for cancellable/asynchronous consumption. What I changed: 1) auth/tactician: new minimal stateful component (Python: redwood_sdk.session.tactician, TS: auth/tactician.ts, Go: session/tactician.go) that tracks token TTLs, schedules a single prefetch per session when TTL < configured window, and exposes hooks for the SDK session factories to opt into warm-up; 2) prefetch policy: defaults to disabled for backwards compatibility, opt-in via SDK session config as prefetch_window_ms and prefetch_enabled; adds telemetry events (tactician.prefetch.scheduled, tactician.prefetch.success, tactician.prefetch.error); 3) PullStream refactor: unified pullstream interface name changes (Python: PullAsync -> PullStreamAsync, TS: PullStream class tidy, Go: PullIterator improvements) and clarified cancellation semantics in all three SDKs; 4) retries interplay: small change to retry predicate so a refresh in-flight counts as a non-retryable transient state for a single attempt to avoid double-refresh races; 5) docs and examples: migration guide, prefetch demo, and updated version notes for Python/TS/Go SDK releases. Compatibility notes: all changes are opt-in and default behavior is preserved; SDK minor bumps included to expose the new options (py 2.7.0, ts 1.18.0, go 1.5.0). Testing: unit tests around tactician scheduling and backoff, integration tests that run a long-lived PullStream while forcing token TTL expiry, and end-to-end scenario against a staging control-plane that simulates delayed token issuance. Rollout plan: feature flagged server-side and SDK flags default off; enable in canary customers after 2 weeks smoke. Checklist: [x] add tactician implementations across three SDKs [x] add telemetry and docs [x] add integration tests [x] add version bumps and changelogs [x] run cross-SDK regression job
text: Context: Cross-SDK follow-up to earlier session/lease work — introduces an "auth tactician" component that centralizes token TTL awareness and a small prefetch policy to avoid tail latency for streamed pull/push APIs. Motivation: customers saw intermittent ~200-400ms spikes when long-running streams triggered last-second token refreshes; retry/backoff logic masked this but increased request churn and observable noise. Goal: reduce tail latency by proactively warming credentials within a TTL window, provide a small, well-documented opt-in prefetch policy across Python/TS/Go SDKs, and clean up PullStream ergonomics so the language SDKs expose a common surface for cancellable/asynchronous consumption. What I changed: 1) auth/tactician: new minimal stateful component (Python: redwood_sdk.session.tactician, TS: auth/tactician.ts, Go: session/tactician.go) that tracks token TTLs, schedules a single prefetch per session when TTL < configured window, and exposes hooks for the SDK session factories to opt into warm-up; 2) prefetch policy: defaults to disabled for backwards compatibility, opt-in via SDK session config as prefetch_window_ms and prefetch_enabled; adds telemetry events (tactician.prefetch.scheduled, tactician.prefetch.success, tactician.prefetch.error); 3) PullStream refactor: unified pullstream interface name changes (Python: PullAsync -> PullStreamAsync, TS: PullStream class tidy, Go: PullIterator improvements) and clarified cancellation semantics in all three SDKs; 4) retries interplay: small change to retry predicate so a refresh in-flight counts as a non-retryable transient state for a single attempt to avoid double-refresh races; 5) docs and examples: migration guide, prefetch demo, and updated version notes for Python/TS/Go SDK releases. Compatibility notes: all changes are opt-in and default behavior is preserved; SDK minor bumps included to expose the new options (py 2.7.0, ts 1.18.0, go 1.5.0). Testing: unit tests around tactician scheduling and backoff, integration tests that run a long-lived PullStream while forcing token TTL expiry, and end-to-end scenario against a staging control-plane that simulates delayed token issuance. Rollout plan: feature flagged server-side and SDK flags default off; enable in canary customers after 2 weeks smoke. title: Tokenwise scale-lowering and ring-buffer collation for quantized kernels
preview:
Summary: Adds a tokenwise scale-lowering compiler pass and a ring-buffer collation stage in the runtime to reduce per-token quant/dequant churn and to coalesce KV cache writes from continuous-batching. Motivation: some workloads with short prefixes and frequent small continuations were suffering from per-token scale conversions and tight kernel launches that prevented microbatch coalescing on INT8 and FP8 execution paths. This PR targets the hot path by lowering scale operations earlier and grouping adjacent KV writes into a ring-backed collator so the GPU-side kernel sees larger contiguous work items without changing model numerics.

What changed (high-level):
- New compiler pass runtime/quant/scale_lowering.cc that detects consecutive token-scale operations and emits a lowered tokenwise scale applied as a fused kernel input where safe. The pass is careful to preserve per-head scaling semantics and falls back to explicit dequant when quantization boundaries are ambiguous.
- Ring-buffer collation runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.cc which accumulates KV cache writes from the continuous-batching queue and emits coalesced write batches to the kernel backend. Collation is implemented lock-free per-GPU-stream and supports configurable watermark/latency tradeoffs.
- Cache eviction hook runtime/cache/evictor.h to ensure collated segments respect existing per-shard eviction windows and do not hold stale KV pointers across rollouts.
- Microbench harness bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py and a small integration test tests/quant/scale_lowering_test.cc that validate numerical parity against the baseline within tolerated quantization noise.
- Docs: docs/perf/quant-collation.md describing knobs, feature flags, and recommended settings for Dedicated and Private deployments.

Commits (summary):
- feat(quant): introduce tokenwise scale-lowering pass (Marina Alvarez)
- perf(scheduler): add ring buffer collator and watermark policy (Marina Alvarez)
- fix(tests): stabilize scale-lowering unit tests and edge-case fallbacks (Marina Alvarez)
- bench: add token collation microbench and CI benchmark job (Marina Alvarez)
- docs: usage and rollout guide for quant collation (Marina Alvarez)

Files touched (representative):
- runtime/quant/scale_lowering.cc, runtime/quant/scale_lowering.h
- runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.cc, runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.h
- runtime/cache/evictor.h
- bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py
- tests/quant/scale_lowering_test.cc
- docs/perf/quant-collation.md

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- Numeric safety: the scale-lowering pass applies a conservative analysis to ensure per-head and per-token scale semantics are preserved. Where the analysis is inconclusive (eg. custom layer-scale hooks or non-standard quant tables), the pass emits an explicit dequant/requant path to preserve correctness. The feature is gated by runtime.quant.scale_lowering and runtime.quant.enable_collation flags.
- Latency vs throughput: ring collator uses a watermark delay (default 1.5ms) to collect contiguous KV writes; on low-concurrency workloads customers can reduce or disable collation to prioritize single-request latency.
- Memory: ring-buffer allocates an ephemeral per-stream buffer sized by max_batch_tokens which defaults to 8k tokens per GPU. This is configurable and documented.

Benchmarks:
- Microbench on A100 (seq_len 2048, batch 8, generation 128): throughput +15% for INT8 end-to-end token/s compared to baseline quant path; median tail latency (p95) increased <2% with default watermark.
- Real workload sim (chat-like prefixes with many small continuations): median tokens/sec +18% and average GPU kernel launches reduced by ~22%. Detailed bench outputs attached in PR artifacts.

Review conversation (high level):
- Daniel Kim (review): "Nice win. Can you add a small test covering per-head scales and the mixed-precision fallback? Also please verify this doesn't regress KV streaming on Dedicated low-memory instances."
- Author reply: "Added per-head unit test and an explicit Dedicated config run in CI. Evictor hook added to avoid stale pointer windows."
- Sofia Nguyen (review): "Please document the feature flags and safe rollout steps; add a metrics counter for collated_batches and collated_tokens."
- Author reply: "Docs added and two counters (quant.collation.batches, quant.collation.tokens) exposed to console and Prometheus."
- Ethan Park (approval): "Bench numbers look solid; tests are green. Recommend squash-merge after CI smoke passes."

CI and validation:
- Unit tests: pass
- Integration (scale path): pass
- Bench job: artifacts show throughput improvements; bench job marked as informational in CI (not gating).
- Lint/format: pass

Rollout plan:
1) Merge behind feature flag off by default.
2) Canary on small Dedicated pool (internal tag: canary-quant-collation) with metrics for collated_batches and kernel_launch_count.
3) Gradual ramp with health checks for tail latency and model-quality regressions (Linear ENG-9273 monitors).

Related: Linear ENG-9273 (quant perf initiative), ENG-9410 (kv cache coherency), SUP-1891 (customer report of small-continuation latency).

Notes for maintainers:
- If future work expands the pass to span inter-layer fusions, ensure the analysis preserves custom kernel hooks (eg. fused FusedFFN variants).
- The ring collator intentionally avoids reordering across tokens belonging to different request IDs; do not change that invariant without consensus.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged after green CI and two approvals.

If you want to test locally: see docs/perf/quant-collation.md and run bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py with --gpu-streams and --watermark-ms to reproduce microbench numbers.

title: adaptive-kernel-selector-and-prefetch-degradation-policy
preview:
Motivation: recent incidents showed increased p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched kernels aggressively for short sequences and simultaneously attempted speculative KV prefetching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel selection policy plus a controlled prefetch degradation path that trades a small amount of throughput for deterministic correctness and lower tail latency under mixed workloads.

What this does (single-string description): Adds a KernelSelector that scores candidate kernels by estimated latency, numerical-safety score, and cache-friendliness; introduces a fallback budget token that bounds how often the fast path can be taken per model+device pair; implements a PrefetchDegradationPolicy that steps down speculative KV prefetch aggressiveness when p95/p99 latency or error-rate thresholds are exceeded; exposes a runtime flag --safe-mode to force conservative kernel and disable micro-prefetch heuristics for regulated workloads. The change coordinates with the continuous-batching layer to emit per-batch scoring metadata so the scheduler can avoid mixing high-risk fastpath batches with conservative ones.

Commits summary: compact, focused commits: 1) kernel selector core + scoring table (kernel_selector.rs, kernel_profiles.json), 2) fallback budget token implementation and metrics, 3) kv prefetch policy and prewarm adjustments (kv/prefetch.rs), 4) scheduler integration (scheduler/batch_manager.rs), 5) unit + microbench + e2e regression tests, 6) docs and changelog.

Files (high-level): runtime/kernel_selector.rs, runtime/kernel_profiles.json, scheduler/batch_manager.rs, kv/prefetch.rs, kv/cache_metrics.rs, serving/continuous_batching.rs, tests/perf/test_kernel_selector.py, tests/e2e/prefetch_degradation.yaml, docs/runtime/fallbacks.md, .github/workflows/perf.yml. Small illustrative snippets are included in the diff where helpful (see kernel selection scoring in runtime/kernel_selector.rs and the degradation thresholds in kv/prefetch.rs).

Performance notes: microbenchmarks on our internal perf-lab (A100 and T4 pools): adaptive selector reduces bad-kernel mispicks by 62% on mixed-length traces; p99 CPU-side tail reduced 18% on A100 with minimal p50 impact. Prefetch degradation cut memory thrash in KV-heavy workloads and removed a class of correctness-sensitive race where prefetch + rapid cache eviction led to stale KV lookups; we instrumented and validated by adding deterministic KV roundtrip tests.

Safety/correctness: numeric-safety score favours kernels with proven fp16/quantized stability for the model; the fallback budget ensures we do not silently oscillate between kernels (avoids thrash). The --safe-mode flag forces: 1) kernel policy to 'conservative', 2) prefetch disabled, 3) strict KV validation on every cache hit (adds latency but improves determinism). Documentation in docs/runtime/fallbacks.md explains when to use safe-mode (regulated/enterprise customers, incident mitigation).

Testing: added unit tests for kernel scoring edge cases, microbench harness to simulate mixed sequence-length streams, and an end-to-end scenario that reproduced the original incident locally. CI matrix covers x86 A100/T4 runners; a flaky perf test (tests/perf/test_batching_latency.py) initially failed and was rerun once. All checks passed on final run: build, lint, unit, microbench, and e2e regression.

Review thread (condensed into single string):
- Ava Chen (review): \"Can we add a telemetric signal for fallback-budget depletion? Want to ensure operators see throttling events.\"
- Leah (author): \"Added metrics: runtime.fallback_budget.used and runtime.fallback_budget.reset_count; documented in metrics.md.\"
- Miguel Torres (review): \"Concerned about memory overhead of prefetch accounting on large-model KV, suggest bounded history window.\"
- Leah: \"Implemented ring-buffer accounting with configurable window; default 128 entries, configurable via env VAR.\"
- Priya Nair (review): \"Please justify safe-mode defaults and add opt-in threshold tuning.\"
- Leah: \"Added configuration docs and conservative defaults that mirror existing SLA targets; included a small utility to translate latency SLOs to prefetch thresholds.\"
- Final: Approvals from Ava and Priya after addressing comments; Miguel requested a follow-up for telemetry retention, tracked in ENG-4210.

CI summary: build ✓, lint ✓, unit tests ✓, microbench ✓, e2e regression ✓ (one flaky perf test rerun). Post-merge: perf dashboards updated automatically via perf-lab webhook to include runtime.fallback_budget.* metrics.

Release notes: \"runtime: add adaptive kernel selection and KV prefetch degradation policy to reduce tail latency and provide a --safe-mode for deterministic deployments. See docs/runtime/fallbacks.md for configuration and operational guidance.\"

Merge: Squash-merged to main after approvals and CI green. Follow-ups: ENG-4210 (telemetry retention), ENG-4299 (experiment: dynamic fallback budget tuning via RL controller).

title: kv-prefetch-aware-batching-with-sparse-scratch-allocator
preview: Context: In production workloads with long chat histories the KV cache dominates transient GPU workspace allocations and forces arena growth during batching spikes. Motivation: Reduce peak GPU footprint while preserving latency SLOs and steady-state throughput. Summary: Introduces a KV-prefetch hotset buffer and a sparse scratch allocator to avoid inflating the global arena during continuous batching. The hotset prefetcher tracks request-level KV hotness and pre-populates a compact per-device hotset capable of serving the majority of cache hits without expanding the scratch arena. The sparse scratch allocator exposes a lightweight free-list for short-lived activation fragments and returns large contiguous ranges back to the arena quickly, reducing fragmentation. Key changes: 1) runtime/kv: prefetch_hotset component with heuristics for prefix/predictive prefetch and bounded memory cap. 2) runtime/allocator: sparse_scratch_allocator that coexists with existing arena and supports deferred materialization for checkpointed activations. 3) scheduler: integrate prefetch decisions into continuous_batcher so requests in the same batch can share prefetched KV and avoid duplicate allocations. 4) observability: new metrics for kv_hotset_hit_ratio, scratch_frag_pct, and prefetch_evictions, plus a dashboard. Results: in internal LLaMA-2-13B quantized chat trace we saw peak GPU RSS drop ~18% (24.6GB -> 20.1GB), 95th latency unchanged within noise, throughput +3% under steady batching, and per-token cost reduction ~12%. Compatibility: falls back to existing arena+kv behavior when hotset cap is exceeded or prefetch disabled. Rollout: feature-flagged, opt-in per-customer; staged rollout with smoke tests and perf canary on dedicated pools. Checklist: [x] unit tests, [x] integration tests, [x] perf regression run, [x] docs, [x] observability. Related: ENG-863 describes requirements for KV costing and ENG-874 is follow-up for multi-GPU hotset federated eviction.

title: introduce-tenant-kms-binding-enforcer-and-audit-linker
preview: Motivation: We need a stronger guardrail that prevents cross-tenant KMS usage in multi-tenant Dedicated/Private deployments while preserving operator ergonomics and auditability. This PR implements a runtime enforcer that ensures KMS key bindings are validated against the request's tenant/namespace claims before allowing encrypt/decrypt/sign operations. It also introduces an audit linker service that enriches existing audit events with KMS provenance (key id, provider, binding-check-result) and a correlation id that ties application requests to KMS calls for post-incident investigation. Design summary: (1) policy-operator plugin that evaluates declared kms.bindings for a tenant namespace and caches validated bindings in a short-lived policy store; (2) request-time gate in the kms-proxy that checks the caller's tenant claim against the policy store and denies calls that violate bindings; (3) audit-linker sidecar that subscribes to internal audit-stream, joins KMS provenance, and emits correlated audit events to the audit-aggregator with a new field audit.kms_correlation_id; (4) feature-flagged rollout behind operator.feature.flags. This description includes a small checklist and rollout plan. Checklist: - [x] policy schema addition and unit tests - [x] kms-proxy binding checks and integration tests - [x] audit-linker implementation and end-to-end tests - [x] migration to grace period for namespaces with no bindings - [x] docs for admin operators and Linear tickets linked. Testing: unit tests for policy compiler, e2e harness using local kms-mock, chaos run for denial scenarios, and load test to ensure latency added is < 3ms p50 for kms-proxy path. Rollout: staged canary (10% traffic) for 4h, 50% for 12h, 100% for 24h; monitor audit link failures, KMS-deny rates, and latency SLOs. Backout: flip feature flag off to revert enforcement; audit-linker safe-mode preserves original audit stream when disabled. Related: ENG-3421 implements the policy schema; SEC-78 is the security review. Links: linear/ENG-3421, security/SEC-78. Notes: No public API changes. Operators will need to declare kms.bindings for existing namespaces or opt into a grace period to avoid sudden denials.

title: Add KMS metrics + tracing spans for standardized wrapper calls
preview: Summary: Instruments all KMS operations invoked via the new shared wrapper with metrics and trace spans following Redwood telemetry conventions. Adds latency histograms, request/error counters (tagged by canonical error taxonomy), and a consistent tracing span shape so on-call can correlate KMS degradation to elevated 5xx/latency. Motivation: As we harden KMS integration paths (ENG-4821), we need consistent KMS dependency signals across control-plane and data-plane components. Recent support cases and a throttling-related incident (SUP-19377) showed we lacked enough granularity to quickly answer: (1) which operation is failing, (2) whether it’s retry amplification, (3) what error class dominates, and (4) which region/provider is impacted. This PR builds on pr-18421’s wrapper API and wires metrics/spans at the wrapper boundary (single place, consistent tags). What’s included: Metrics (emitted on every wrapper call): 1) redwood.kms.request.count (counter) tags: operation, provider, region, result (success|error), error_category (when error). 2) redwood.kms.request.latency_ms (histogram) tags: operation, provider, region, result. 3) redwood.kms.retries.count (counter) tags: operation, provider, region, error_category. 4) redwood.kms.throttled.count (counter) tags: provider, region (incremented for throttling-class responses, including SDK-specific throttle codes mapped into the canonical taxonomy). Tracing: Adds a span around each KMS wrapper call: span name: kms.<operation> (e.g., kms.decrypt, kms.encrypt, kms.generate_data_key, kms.describe_key) attributes: kms.operation, kms.provider, cloud.region, kms.key_ref (redacted; alias name when present, otherwise stable hash), kms.retry_attempt (per attempt), kms.error_category (when error), kms.http_status (when available from SDK metadata), and kms.request_id (when provided by provider SDK). Notes: we explicitly do not attach raw key ARNs or plaintext key IDs; key references are sanitized. Error taxonomy mapping: Uses the canonical categories discussed in ENG-4822 and the eng-security thread (AccessDenied, NotFound, Throttling, Timeout, InvalidCiphertext, DependencyUnavailable, Unknown). Mapping is implemented in kms/telemetry/error_mapping.go and shared by metrics + span attributes + logs. Sampling: Spans respect existing tracing sampling decisions (no new always-on sampling); metrics are always emitted. This is intentional so we can alert on dependency health even when traces are sampled. Changed areas (high level): - pkg/security/kms/wrapper/*: inject TelemetryReporter into wrapper client; emit metrics + spans; ensure metrics are recorded for success/failure and for each attempt where relevant. - pkg/telemetry/metrics/registry.go: registers KMS meters and canonical tag keys (prevents ad-hoc tag drift). - pkg/telemetry/tracing/spans.go: helper to build KMS spans with consistent attribute names and safe key redaction. - pkg/security/kms/providers/aws.go, gcp.go, azure.go: normalizes provider-specific error codes into canonical error categories; plumbs request-id and status metadata when available. - docs/internal/observability/metrics.md: adds KMS metric names + tag glossary (internal only). Example metric/tag conventions (for reviewers): - operation values: decrypt | encrypt | generate_data_key | describe_key | reencrypt - provider values: aws | gcp | azure - error_category values: AccessDenied | NotFound | Throttling | Timeout | InvalidCiphertext | DependencyUnavailable | Unknown - region values: us-east-1, etc. (or provider equivalent) Testing: - Unit tests for error mapping across providers (golden table tests). - Unit tests verifying tag sets (ensures we don’t regress into high-cardinality tags). - Local smoke: ran data-plane integration tests against the KMS stub; verified metrics emission via in-process exporter. Rollout / follow-ups: - SRE will add dashboards/alerts in a separate PR (observability-pack/pr-844). - Once wrapper migration is complete across remaining call sites, we can remove legacy ad-hoc KMS logging in services. Checklist: - [x] No sensitive data in metrics/tags (no key ARNs, no account IDs) - [x] Error taxonomy matches ENG-4822 - [x] Metrics follow Redwood naming + tagging conventions - [x] Traces use consistent span naming/attributes - [x] Unit tests for mapping and tag set validation

title: Cost‑Smarts: telemetry-driven prefill scheduler and quantization hint adapter
preview:
Context and motivation: Large parts of our cost-automation stack rely on static prefill and quantization defaults that are tuned per-model but not per-workload. Customers running mixed telemetry (short chat bursts + long completion bursts) see suboptimal unit economics because prefill and quant decisions are not being informed by runtime signals. This PR introduces 'Cost‑Smarts' — a telemetry-driven prefill scheduler plus a quantization hint adapter that surfaces unit-econ deltas into the serving runtime policy engine.

What this change does: Implements a small, runtime-side scheduler that consumes existing request telemetry (sequence length histograms, prefix-kv hit ratios, tail-latency percentile) and emits short-lived prefill decisions (aggressive/standard/disabled) for a given route+model tuple. In parallel, the quantization hint adapter exposes a per-route score representing the estimated per-token cost uplift vs quality risk, which the router can use to select a compatible quantized variant when under cost pressure. Both components default to conservative behavior and are configurable from the runtime config and via the console.

Design highlights and tradeoffs: The scheduler is intentionally lightweight — it aggregates telemetry in 10s windows and uses rule-based heuristics derived from recent econ experiments (prefill when median prefix length > 16 and kv-hit < 0.6, or when 95p latency < configured threshold). We intentionally avoid ML models for initial rollout to keep behavior explainable and safe. The quant hint adapter computes a rollout-friendly score using a small lookup table based on model family, qprofile, and observed batch sizes; the score is a float in [0.0, 1.0] representing 'cost-favorability'.

Checklist: - [x] Runtime scheduler + config knobs - [x] Quant hint adapter + API contract with router - [x] Console defaults/UI flag (defaults panel) - [x] End-to-end unit and integration tests - [x] Telemetry metrics + dashboards - [x] Docs and migration notes

Files and high-level changes: Added a new runtime module runtime/prefill_scheduler.rs implementing the aggregator and decision API; runtime/quant_hint_adapter.rs implements the lightweight scoring; modified router/policy_router.go to accept quant_hint scores when selecting a variant; console defaults UI tweak in console/src/routes/costDefaults; new telemetry metrics in observability/metrics.yaml and a Grafana dashboard JSON in observability/dashboards/prefill_cost_smarts.json. Tests added under runtime/tests and router/integration.

Safety and rollout: Defaults are conservative. The system starts in passive mode (observing only) unless enabled by a new env var PREFILL_SCHEDULER_MODE=active. New config 'costSmarts' allows per-tenant opt-in and pinning of decisions. We added automatic fallback to existing prefill/quant defaults in all code paths.

Performance and unit economics: Internal benchmarks on a mixed workload trace (chat + long completions) show a 7–12% reduction in per-token GPU seconds when cost-smarts is active + quant variants available. Tail latency impact was within measurement noise in 95th percentile tests when prefill choices leaned conservative.

Migration notes: Operators can enable cost-smarts per-tenant via the console or config. No changes needed to existing customer configs unless they want to opt in to aggressive prefill or quant-favoring behavior.

Related work: complements recent econ autotuners (ENG-2201) and the prefill-prober; differs by being runtime-native and telemetry-driven rather than offline profile-based.

How to review: Start with runtime/prefill_scheduler.rs for decision logic, then router/policy_router.go for selection changes. Console changes are small and behind a feature flag. Tests include unit coverage for heuristics and an integration scenario that replays a production trace.

Summary: Implements a safe, incremental path to using live telemetry to improve prefill/quant decisions and lower per-token cost without changing public inference APIs.

title: Dedicated autoscaler: add metrics + structured logs for desired vs bounded capacity and prediction error
preview:
Context: As part of Dedicated autoscaling bounds + predictive mode, we need auditability and operability around what the autoscaler *wanted* to do vs what it actually did after applying customer bounds (min/max) and safety invariants. This PR adds first-class metrics and structured logs for (1) requested desired replicas vs bounded desired replicas, (2) bound-hit counters (clamped to min/max), (3) prediction error and staleness when predictive mode is enabled, and (4) an explicit scale-event reason enum that shows up in both metrics and logs.

What’s in this PR:
- New gauge metrics (emitted every autoscaler tick per deployment/region):
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.desired_replicas (the raw desired output from the decision function)
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.bounded_desired_replicas (after applying min/max + safety floors)
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.current_replicas
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.queue_depth (existing signal re-emitted consistently for dashboards)
- New counters:
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.bound_hit_total{bound="min"|"max"} increments when desired is clamped
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.scale_event_total{direction="up"|"down", reason=*} increments on actual scale actions
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.prediction_stale_total increments when a forecast is ignored due to staleness
- New histograms (guarded by config to avoid extra cardinality):
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.prediction_error_ratio (|predicted - actual| / max(actual,1))
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.decision_latency_ms (internal time to compute desired)
- Structured decision logs (one per tick) with stable field names for searchability and compliance reviews. Key fields:
  - deployment_id, customer_id, region
  - policy_min, policy_max, predictive_enabled
  - desired_replicas, bounded_desired_replicas, current_replicas
  - scale_action (none|up|down), scale_delta
  - reason (enum), reason_detail (short string)
  - predictor_used (true/false), predictor_age_seconds

Scale reason enum (used consistently across metric label + log field):
- reactive_queue_depth
- reactive_saturation
- predictive_forecast
- bounded_by_max
- bounded_by_min
- cooldown_active
- no_change
- invalid_prediction

Cardinality / labeling:
- All metrics are labeled with deployment_id and region. customer_id is included only in structured logs (NOT as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality blowups. This matches the current fleet metrics policy.
- reason labels are limited to the enum above (no freeform strings).

Illustrative example (structured log):
{"msg":"autoscaler_tick","deployment_id":"ded-9d2f...","region":"us-east-1","policy_min":4,"policy_max":24,"predictive_enabled":true,"desired_replicas":31,"bounded_desired_replicas":24,"current_replicas":20,"scale_action":"up","scale_delta":4,"reason":"bounded_by_max","reason_detail":"desired=31 clamped to max=24","predictor_used":true,"predictor_age_seconds":58}

Tests:
- Unit tests for reason selection (predictive vs reactive overrides) to ensure we don’t emit conflicting reason labels.
- Integration-style test for clamp behavior to ensure bound_hit_total increments exactly once per tick when clamping occurs.

Follow-ups (out of this PR):
- Grafana dashboard panels + alert rules live in observability-pack PRs.
- Control plane audit event schema (who/when/what) is handled in the audit-events PR.

Rollout:
- Metrics are behind the existing dedicated_autoscaling_bounds feature gate. When disabled, no new bounded/predictive metrics are emitted.
- Prediction histograms default off in prod until we validate cost/volume during pilot (config: autoscaler.emit_prediction_histograms=false).

Refs: ENG-22143, ENG-22140. Also informs debugging for SUP-28479 (pilot oscillation) by exposing scale_event reasons and predictor staleness.
